Bridging the Gap Between Data Reproduction and Prediction: The
Impact of Feature Selection and Cross-Validation Strategies on
Prediction of Ambient Ultrafine Particles Collected with Mobile
Monitoring

Milad Saeedi, Jad Zalzal, Arman Ganji, Junshi Xu, Sebastian D. Goodfellow, and Marianne Hatzopoulou*

Cite This: https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.est.5c12601
Read Online

ACCESS
Metrics & More
Article Recommendations
*
sı
Supporting Information

ABSTRACT: Reliable exposure assessment is vital for epidemio-
logical research, but weaknesses in land-use regression (LUR)
models undermine its validity. Using mobile ultrafine particle
(UFP) data in Toronto, we compared LUR models trained under
random, spatial, temporal, and spatiotemporal cross-validation
(CV), with and without forward feature selection (FFS). Model
hyperparameters and feature subsets were optimized within each
CV scheme. Spatial CV folds were designed at fine scales to reflect
UFP autocorrelation.

File: bridging-the-gap-between-data-reproduction-and-prediction-the-impact-of-feature-selection-and-cross-validation.pdf
Document type: research_paper
Page header: pubs.acs.org/est | Article
Page: 1

d without forward feature selection (FFS). Model
hyperparameters and feature subsets were optimized within each
CV scheme. Spatial CV folds were designed at fine scales to reflect
UFP autocorrelation. Each approach was evaluated on a hold-out
test set, across CV schemes, and against independent stationary
backyard measurements. Models based on spatiotemporal CV
coupled with FFS were able to reduce overfitting, improve
generalization, and produce stable exposure surfaces. These
surfaces avoided the spatial artifacts and exaggerated variable effects typically seen in models trained with random CV. Models
tuned with random CV overfit, performed poorly on independent samples, and were sensitive to outliers. The average percentage
error (APE) decreased from ∼217% for a model with random-CV to ∼79% with spatiotemporal CV and FFS. Our findings
demonstrate that proper alignment of model design with the data’s spatiotemporal structure and modeling objective ensures

1. INTRODUCTION

Air pollution is a major public health concern, especially in urban
areas.1−3 Accurately assessing exposure necessitates advanced
measurement and modeling methods, including mobile
monitoring, remote sensing, land-use regression (LUR), and
machine learning (ML).4,5 LUR modeling is widely used to
estimate air pollution exposure in urban areas by leveraging
geographic and environmental predictors to map pollutant

se regression (LUR), and
machine learning (ML).4,5 LUR modeling is widely used to
estimate air pollution exposure in urban areas by leveraging
geographic and environmental predictors to map pollutant
concentrations at fine scales. Historically, LUR relied on data
from a limited number of fixed monitoring stations,6,7 which
constrained its ability to capture localized variations.8 Recent
progress has broadened LUR’s scope, incorporating advanced
data collection methods and diverse predictors. Machine
learning approaches further enhance predictive accuracy.9,10

Mobile monitoring enhances exposure assessment for
spatially varying air pollutants by covering many more locations
and capturing the large spatial variability characteristic of urban
areas.11 When enabled by portable low-cost sensors on vehicles,
it also offers high-resolution measurements over wide areas with
fewer devices.12 Mobile air quality measurements are often
conducted at specific times and under varying meteorological

particles (UFP) differ markedly from other pollutants such as
PM2.5 or NO2 in both their physical behavior and chemical
reactivity.15 Emitted primarily from traffic sources, UFPs have a
high surface-area-to-mass ratio that enhances their volatility and
capacity to adsorb or desorb semivolatile compounds, resulting
in rapid transformation and growth into larger particles in the
atmosphere.16 Because of their short atmospheric lifetime and
strong dependence on local traffic and meteorological
conditions, UFPs exhibit steep spatial gradients and high

might simply “memorize” the specific conditions of the sampling

For instance, Blanco et al. (2023)24 demonstrated that different

that campaigns with temporal restrictions (limited to business
hours, rush hours, or specific seasons) reduced model
performance and produced different spatial surfaces. While
they suggest improving sampling strategies to enhance data

ctions (limited to business

Model selection and validation are crucial for building reliable
LUR models for air pollution.5 A common approach for tuning a
model is random cross-validation (CV). Random CV is
straightforward but often overestimates performance in spatially
or temporally structured data, as nearby locations or time
periods can leak between training and test sets, inflating
performance metrics.25−27 This limitation arises because
conventional random CV assumes that observations are
independent and identically distributed, a core assumption
underpinning its validity as an unbiased estimator of out-of-

File: bridging-the-gap-between-data-reproduction-and-prediction-the-impact-of-feature-selection-and-cross-validation.pdf
Document type: research_paper
Page header: Environmental Science & Technology | pubs.acs.org/est | Article
Page: 2

limitation arises because
conventional random CV assumes that observations are
independent and identically distributed, a core assumption
underpinning its validity as an unbiased estimator of out-of-
sample error.28−30 In spatial or temporal data, this assumption is
violated, observations are not independent, leading to
information leakage between folds and overly optimistic
performance estimates.31−33 The spatial and temporal autocor-
relation in air pollution data may render random splitting
inappropriate, risking overfitting to noncausal predictors and
over optimistic performance metrics. Therefore, more advanced
strategies are needed to avoid overfitting.34,35 Roberts et al.
(2017)36 recommend block CV for model selection, ensuring
data are partitioned by spatial or temporal structures. Blocking
by location or time provides a stringent test of whether a model
can predict in completely unseen regions or periods. Indeed,
using appropriate CV helps select models and hyperparameters

es. Blocking
by location or time provides a stringent test of whether a model
can predict in completely unseen regions or periods. Indeed,
using appropriate CV helps select models and hyperparameters
that avoid overfitting to spurious correlations present in training
data.36,37

The accuracy of LUR models depends on predictor variables,
which capture built environment and other factors influencing
air pollution levels.38,39 It is common to explore a wide array of
potential predictor variables when constructing LUR mod-
els.40−43 However, excessive inclusion of predictor variables can
lead to overfitting, reducing model generalizability and
predictive performance.34,44,45 As a result, feature selection is a
critical step in LUR. Various feature selection methods,
including forward stepwise linear regression46−48 and
LASSO,49 have been used to improve LUR performance and
reduce overfitting. Forward feature selection (FFS) is an

File: bridging-the-gap-between-data-reproduction-and-prediction-the-impact-of-feature-selection-and-cross-validation.pdf
Document type: research_paper
Page header: Environmental Science & Technology | pubs.acs.org/est | Article
Page: 2

rious feature selection methods,
including forward stepwise linear regression46−48 and
LASSO,49 have been used to improve LUR performance and
reduce overfitting. Forward feature selection (FFS) is an
iterative procedure that adds variables sequentially based on
their contribution to model performance. In each step, variables
are evaluated for significance and multicollinearity, excluding
redundant or highly correlated land-use predictors. However,
feature selection methods such as FFS cannot remove spurious
predictors under random CV, where spatially dependent
observations appear in both training and test sets.44,50,51 This
can inflate performance metrics, leading to overfitted models

and artifactual spatial patterns that fail to generalize across new
regions or time periods. More broadly, these risks stem from
reliance on purely statistical criteria that may omit causal
relationships. This leads to selecting variables that appear

alize across new
regions or time periods. More broadly, these risks stem from
reliance on purely statistical criteria that may omit causal
relationships. This leads to selecting variables that appear
significant by chance but do not generalize in autocorrelated
data, generating misleading models.51−54 As the number of
candidate variables increases, the likelihood of selecting
variables that appear statistically significant by chance but do
not contribute to generalizable predictions also increases.55

Studies demonstrate that selected variables may be significant by
chance but fail to generalize in spatially autocorrelated data.56,57

To address these limitations, this study explores several
modeling frameworks to evaluate the approaches that can best
mitigate overfitting in mobile monitoring data. For example, in
one framework, we examine the integration of FFS with
spatiotemporal CV to assess how aligning feature selection

proaches that can best
mitigate overfitting in mobile monitoring data. For example, in
one framework, we examine the integration of FFS with
spatiotemporal CV to assess how aligning feature selection
and hyperparameter tuning with the data’s spatial and temporal
structure influences model generalization.

Robust models for data with spatial and temporal
autocorrelation require CV strategies for hyperparameter tuning
and feature selection to ensure generalizability and minimize
overfitting.37,58 Meyer et al. (2019)44 showed that combining
spatial CV with FFS significantly improved model robustness by
avoiding spurious predictors in spatially correlated data.
Similarly, Meyer et al. (2018)52 integrated FFS with a target-
oriented spatiotemporal CV approach for temperature and soil-
content data from stationary stations, systematically removing
misleading variables and achieving more generalizable pre-
dictions. Baumberger et al. (2024)59 highlighted the importance

ure and soil-

In this study, we develop LUR models using Extreme
Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) to predict UFP concentrations
based on mobile monitoring data collected in Toronto. We
systematically compare seven modeling approaches to assess the
impact of hyperparameter tuning and feature selection on model
generalizability. In addition, we develop four additional models
to assess the robustness of modeling approaches under different
outlier-handling methods. We tested CV strategies for tuning
and feature selection and assessed model robustness under
different outlier treatments. This allowed us to compare

roaches under different

and feature selection and assessed model robustness under
different outlier treatments. This allowed us to compare
modeling approaches and understand how they influence
performance when dealing with mobile monitoring data. This
study contributes to the development of machine learning-based
exposure assessment models, providing insights into optimizing
feature selection, hyperparameter tuning, and CV strategies to
improve the reliability of mobile air pollution estimates. Finally,
we compare model predictions against continuously recorded
data from stationary sensors.

2. MATERIALS AND METHODS

2.1. Study Area and Data Collection

In Toronto, UFP concentrations are primarily influenced by traffic
emissions, particularly from diesel vehicles and congestion along major
roadways, as well as localized industrial activities.60 These sources
create strong spatial gradients and diurnal variation, with higher

roadways, as well as localized industrial activities.60 These sources
create strong spatial gradients and diurnal variation, with higher
concentrations during rush hours and lower levels at night and on
weekends.5 Dilution and dispersion in green and open areas such as
parks and vegetated corridors further reduce local UFP levels through
enhanced atmospheric mixing and particle deposition.61 Between April
7 and June 23, 2021, we conducted mobile monitoring in Toronto to
capture spatial variations in UFP concentrations between 8 AM and 7

2.2. Predictor Variables
To ensure spatial consistency, we defined 100 m distance points along
the mobile monitoring routes. At each of these points, air pollution
measurements [49] were aggregated and a median value reported.64

Spatial buffers were then created around these 100 m interval points to
extract land use and traffic-related variables within predefined buffer
zones. To model UFP concentrations, we incorporated a compre-

extract land use and traffic-related variables within predefined buffer
zones. To model UFP concentrations, we incorporated a compre-
hensive set of 194 predictor variables, categorized into land use, traffic-
related, distance-based, and temporally changing variables (Table S1).
2.3. Model Development
We used XGBoost, a gradient boosting decision tree algorithm, to
develop the LUR models for UFP. Outlier removal by road type was
applied using the Interquartile Range (IQR) method, where outliers
were identified separately for each road type and removed if they fell
outside Q1 −1.5 × IQR and Q3 + 1.5 × IQR. Finally, normalization
was applied to standardize continuous predictors. From this data set, we
created seven models to evaluate different CV strategies for
hyperparameter tuning and feature selection. In addition, we explored
two alternative outlier-handling techniques: removing values above the

eated seven models to evaluate different CV strategies for
hyperparameter tuning and feature selection. In addition, we explored
two alternative outlier-handling techniques: removing values above the
95th percentile or retaining all spikes, by building four additional
models following two modeling frameworks: random CV alone, and
spatiotemporal CV combined with feature selection.

We used 80% of the data points as training set for hyperparameter
tuning and feature selection. All model development and validation
procedures were implemented in Python using the scikit-learn and
XGBoost libraries.

We defined four CV schemes for model tuning, each using K-fold CV
on the training data set.

• Random CV (RCV): Data were randomly split into training and
validation (10-folds), ignoring spatial or temporal structure.
• Spatial CV (SCV): We generated 400 geographic clusters using
the 100 m-aggregated training records (see Figure S1a). These

• Spatiotemporal CV (STCV): We combined spatial and
temporal blocking by holding out data that were independent
in both space and time (step-by-step details are provided in the
SI). In our implementation, we ensured that no location from a
certain set of clusters on certain days appeared in training if it
was in the validation set. Essentially, we withheld entire day-of-
cluster combinations. This STCV approach is very stringent: for
example, the model might be trained on data from most
locations and days but tested on data from a particular group of
locations on specific days (neither those locations nor dates
appear in training). Because our data set is large, we achieved
STCV by first clustering locations (as in SCV) and then in each

File: bridging-the-gap-between-data-reproduction-and-prediction-the-impact-of-feature-selection-and-cross-validation.pdf
Document type: research_paper
Page header: Environmental Science & Technology | pubs.acs.org/est | Article
Page: 4

group of
locations on specific days (neither those locations nor dates
appear in training). Because our data set is large, we achieved
STCV by first clustering locations (as in SCV) and then in each
fold selecting a subset of clusters and specific days to hold out,
such that validation data are separated in both dimensions. This
approach aligns with the “target-oriented” validation concept
introduced by Meyer et al. (2018).52

For each CV scheme, we conducted hyperparameter tuning (HP) by
Bayesian optimization using only the training folds and evaluated
performance on the validation fold in each iteration. Hyperparameters
such as tree depth, learning rate, and regularization parameters were
optimized based on the average validation performance across the folds.

In addition to the full-feature models, we built models with FFS
under the three structured CV frameworks (see SI for details). We
employed a forward stepwise selection approach: we added predictors

In addition to the full-feature models, we built models with FFS
under the three structured CV frameworks (see SI for details). We
employed a forward stepwise selection approach: we added predictors
one at a time, in each step including the variable that most improved the
CV mean squared error (MSE) under the given CV scheme. This
process was repeated until adding any remaining variable did not
increase the MSE. The feature selection was performed within each CV
strategy (see SI for details). This ensured that selected predictors

contributed to generalizable skill rather than fitting coincidences of the
training data.44,52 Figure 2 presents a conceptual diagram of the STCV
coupled with FFS modeling framework, illustrating the iterative
workflow from spatiotemporal fold definition and hyperparameter
tuning to feature selection and final model evaluation. While this
schematic highlights the methodological innovation specific to the

e
workflow from spatiotemporal fold definition and hyperparameter
tuning to feature selection and final model evaluation. While this

STCV−FFS framework, the same forward feature selection workflow
was also applied under the spatial and temporal CV schemes, with fold

Ultimately, we trained and evaluated 11 models (Table 1) to
examine how modeling frameworks (including hyperparameter tuning
and feature selection) and outlier treatment influence model perform-
ance and robustness. First, we evaluated seven modeling frameworks:
RCV_HP, SCV_HP, TCV_HP, STCV_HP, SCV_FFS, TCV_FFS,
and STCV_FFS which differ in their hyperparameter tuning and feature
selection processes. Next, to examine how outlier handling and
sampling changes affect the RCV_HP and STCV_FFS frameworks,
four additional models were developed: RCV_HP_95,
STCV_FFS_95, RCV_HP_noOut, STCV_FFS_noOut. Notably,

, to examine how outlier handling and

2.4. Model Implementation and Evaluation

reflect model performance and generalizability in air pollution exposure
assessment studies.35,36,44 Given the spatial and temporal complexities

used a 20% random holdout sample excluded from training, tuning, and
feature selection. We computed median concentrations to mitigate
transient spikes. This data set, consistently used across all models,
provided a standardized comparison basis, reporting coefficient of

Squared Error (RMSE) as evaluation metrics (see SI for details).
Second, to assess whether a modeling framework improved general-
ization, we reallocated all data into random, spatial, temporal, and
spatiotemporal folds. The tuning and feature selection process relied on
a specific data split, whereas final split for checking the models were
developed using the entire data set with different cluster allocations.

Given our modeling context, the interpretation of CV metrics,
especially R2, requires caution. Mobile monitoring data aggregated from
limited visits per location is susceptible to episodic pollution spikes and
temporal variability, potentially inflating prediction errors and lowering
R2, especially under rigorous CV schemes (SCV, TCV, STCV).
Consequently, while R2 is essential for seeing the improvement in
generalization, it does not necessarily represent the model’s capability
to accurately estimate average pollutant levels over a given period. A
model evaluated under TCV, for example, might exhibit low R2 due to
temporal variability but still accurately capture average spatial pollution

File: bridging-the-gap-between-data-reproduction-and-prediction-the-impact-of-feature-selection-and-cross-validation.pdf

Page: 5

stimate average pollutant levels over a given period. A
model evaluated under TCV, for example, might exhibit low R2 due to
temporal variability but still accurately capture average spatial pollution
patterns when evaluated on continuous data representative of the given
period.23,25,47,65 In this study, CV performance metrics (e.g., R2, MSE)
were primarily used for hyperparameter tuning and feature selection
and then each model was tested across four CV. Overall, by integrating
rigorous CV for evaluation (to inform robust model development and
prevent overfitting) and an internal holdout sample evaluation, we aim
to provide a balanced, transparent, and realistic assessment of each
model’s capability to generalize.

3. RESULTS

3.1. Measured Concentrations
The mobile monitoring campaign yielded an extensive data set of UFP
measurements (over 440,000 valid observations after quality control).
Concentrations varied greatly from day to day, reflecting changing
traffic, meteorology, and local emissions (Figure S4). To further
characterize the data set, we first examined the distribution of UFP
concentrations after applying IQR-based filtering by road type. This
method excluded road-specific statistical outliers and resulted in a mean
concentration of 16,817 particles/cm3 and a median of 13,856
particles/cm3. We also assessed the impact of two alternative outlier

File: bridging-the-gap-between-data-reproduction-and-prediction-the-impact-of-feature-selection-and-cross-validation.pdf
Document type: research_paper
Page header: Environmental Science & Technology | pubs.acs.org/est | Article
Page: 6

d excluded road-specific statistical outliers and resulted in a mean
concentration of 16,817 particles/cm3 and a median of 13,856
particles/cm3. We also assessed the impact of two alternative outlier
strategies: retaining all values without removal and excluding the top 5%
of concentrations (95th percentile removal). The unfiltered data set
yielded the highest mean (23,906 particles/cm3) and a median of
14,777 particles/cm3, indicating a right-skewed distribution influenced
by extreme values. The 95th percentile removal approach produced
similar summary statistics to the IQR method, with a mean of 16,816
particles/cm3 and a median of 14,017 particles/cm3. These results,
summarized in Table S2, highlight how different filtering strategies can
shape the overall distribution of mobile UFP data.

Measurements from the backyard sensors over the three-week
monitoring period (Figure S5) reveal substantial variability in UFP
concentrations across locations, likely reflecting differences in

ata.

Measurements from the backyard sensors over the three-week
monitoring period (Figure S5) reveal substantial variability in UFP
concentrations across locations, likely reflecting differences in

proximity to emission sources such as roads, buildings, or vegetation.
Summary statistics for each monitor (Table S3) further highlight this
heterogeneity: median UFP concentrations ranged from as low as 2915
particles/cm3 to as high as 9018 particles/cm3. Compared to mobile
monitoring data, with a median concentration of approximately 13,856
particles/cm3 (after IQR-based filtering), stationary measurements
exhibited lower medians and reduced day-to-day variability.

4. DISCUSSION

In this study, two internal evaluation approaches were used: a
20% random holdout and four cross-validation schemes, which
were applied to assess each LUR model using mobile monitoring
data. Because mobile data are collected over short periods and
can exhibit high temporal and spatial variability, these internal
evaluations may not fully capture true long-term pollution levels.
In addition, structured CV can impose overly stringent spatial
and temporal constraints, creating test scenarios that are more
challenging than typical real-world conditions and may not
accurately reflect a model’s true predictive power. Therefore, the
metrics derived from mobile data under different CV schemes

File: bridging-the-gap-between-data-reproduction-and-prediction-the-impact-of-feature-selection-and-cross-validation.pdf
Document type: research_paper
Page header: Environmental Science & Technology | pubs.acs.org/est | Article
Page: 8

that are more
challenging than typical real-world conditions and may not
accurately reflect a model’s true predictive power. Therefore, the
metrics derived from mobile data under different CV schemes
should not be interpreted as indicators of each model’s ability to
predict longer-term concentrations, which is the ultimate goal of
modeling. Instead, they should be used to compare robustness,
overfitting, and improvement in generalization among the
models. Accordingly, the reported R2 values should be
interpreted in a comparative sense, highlighting relative model
stability across validation strategies rather than as absolute
measures of predictive accuracy.

4.1. Impact of Cross Validation for Hyperparameter Tuning

The choice of CV for tuning and feature selection had a large
impact on model performance and generalizability. Models
tuned with conventional random CV appeared to fit the training
data extremely well but failed to generalize to unseen space or
time.

impact on model performance and generalizability. Models

time. For instance, RCV_HP achieved an almost perfect fit to its
training data (R2 ≈0.999) but saw its R2 drop to ∼0.71 on a
hold-out test set. This sharp decline, alongside a jump in error
measures, illustrates severe overfitting when spatial and
temporal autocorrelations are ignored during hyperparameter
tuning. In contrast, models tuned under structured CV scheme
yielded more moderate training fits (training R2 ∼0.54 to 0.83)
and comparable test performance (test R2 ∼0.51 to 0.66).

4.2. Feature Selection Combined with CV Methods and
Predictor Importance

FFS further enhanced model generalizability and interpret-
ability, especially when coupled with structured CV. Without
feature selection, even a spatially blocked model could retain
spurious predictors and overfit certain conditions. For example,
the Spatial CV model with all features (SCV_HP) and even with
FFS (SCV_FFS) showed relatively high training R2 (∼0.95)
alongside a lower test R2 (∼0.72), suggesting some overfitting
persisted.

Our study focuses on generalizable prediction, aiming to

the sampled domain. Unlike spatiotemporal interpolation

transferable relationships between predictors and pollution

4.3. Impact of Outlier Treatment
Handling of extreme UFP readings (“outliers”) had a notable
effect on models tuned with random CV but had minimal impact
on those built with the spatiotemporal CV + feature selection
framework. When using random CV for model tuning, the
inclusion or exclusion of transient extreme spikes significantly
altered both error metrics and predicted spatial patterns,
indicating a tendency to “chase” these peaks. In contrast, the
models developed with spatiotemporal CV and feature selection
demonstrated a remarkable robustness to outlier handling. The
STCV_FFS models performed nearly identically regardless of
whether we kept all spikes or filtered them out. For these models,
when predictions were compared with stationary measurements,
the errors remained consistently low across all outlier treat-
ments, indicating that the model had learned the underlying
signal in the data. Despite this improved performance, the

ary measurements,
the errors remained consistently low across all outlier treat-
ments, indicating that the model had learned the underlying
signal in the data. Despite this improved performance, the
STCV_FFS, STCV_FFS_95, and STCV_FFS_noOut models
still tended to overpredict relative to stationary backyard
monitors, resulting in a mean absolute percentage error of
about 78%−79%. This discrepancy is expected because mobile
monitoring measurements were collected directly on roadways,
whereas stationary monitors are typically located in residential
backyards farther from emission sources. UFP concentrations
are known to decrease steeply with distance from traffic, often
dropping by 50%−70% within the first 100−300 m and
approaching background levels beyond 300 m.67,68 Therefore,
higher predicted values compared to backyard observations are
physically consistent with the well-established near-road decay
behavior of UFPs. The robustness of the STCV_FFS framework

,68 Therefore,
higher predicted values compared to backyard observations are
physically consistent with the well-established near-road decay
behavior of UFPs. The robustness of the STCV_FFS framework
to outliers is driven by its ability to remove predictors that do not
meaningfully contribute to persistent patterns, while retaining
those linked to stable sources such as traffic and road networks.
This property is especially important for exposure mapping: it
means the model can incorporate genuine high-pollution events
(e.g., data recorded while passing a truck or during high traffic
hours) into the predictions without being misled by them. This
guarantees that true extreme pollution events are kept and
correctly shown as hotspots if they are part of consistent
patterns, such as persistently high concentrations along highway
interchanges.

4.4. Modeling Recommendations
Based on our findings and previous studies, we propose a set of
best practices for developing generalizable models using
autocorrelated air quality data collected via mobile modeling.

File: bridging-the-gap-between-data-reproduction-and-prediction-the-impact-of-feature-selection-and-cross-validation.pdf
Document type: research_paper
Page header: Environmental Science & Technology | pubs.acs.org/est | Article
Page: 10

Define the Modeling Objective: Clearly distinguish between
interpolation (within-sample prediction) and generalization
(prediction at new locations or times), as this determines how
training and validation data are split.

Align Cross-Validation with the Modeling Goal and Data
Structure: The structure of CV and the definition of folds should
reflect both the study objective and the data’s spatiotemporal
correlation structure.

Design Folds Based on Autocorrelation Range: Spatial folds
and cluster sizes should reflect the data’s autocorrelation range,
with buffer zones applied around validation clusters to prevent
spatial leakage.

Integrate CV Into Feature Selection and Tuning: Perform
feature selection and hyperparameter tuning within the
structured CV to prevent overfitting and the selection of
spurious predictors tied to specific sampling patterns.

Prioritize External and Visual Validation: For mobile data,
when the goal is to estimate average surfaces over time, rely on

File: bridging-the-gap-between-data-reproduction-and-prediction-the-impact-of-feature-selection-and-cross-validation.pdf
Document type: research_paper
Page header: Environmental Science & Technology | pubs.acs.org/est | Article
Page: 10

the selection of
spurious predictors tied to specific sampling patterns.

Prioritize External and Visual Validation: For mobile data,
when the goal is to estimate average surfaces over time, rely on
independent continuous measurements and visual inspection to
confirm expected spatial trends and detect artifacts. Internal CVs
using mobile data should not be interpreted as performance
indicators. When comparing several models, internal CV metrics
should be interpreted comparatively to assess robustness and
overfitting. Random CV reflects the model’s fit within the
sampled data, while structured CV (spatial, temporal, or
spatiotemporal) provides a more realistic measure of general-
ization. Models that maintain more balanced metrics across
these CV schemes generally demonstrate stronger real-world
reliability.

4.5. Future Directions and Implications

Future work should investigate the transferability of the feature
selection approach combined with spatiotemporal CV by

Page: 10

rate stronger real-world
reliability.

4.5. Future Directions and Implications

Future work should investigate the transferability of the feature
selection approach combined with spatiotemporal CV by
applying the model to different time periods and additional
urban areas. Testing the model’s performance on multiyear
mobile monitoring data sets in diverse cities or time periods
would help determine whether the identified predictors remain
robust over time and across various urban contexts. Future work
could also test the applicability of this framework to other
pollutants such as NO2 or PM2.5, which spatial and temporal
characteristics differ from UFPs. For such pollutants, the spatial
folds in blocked cross-validation should be defined according to
the range and structure of their respective autocorrelation
patterns. Another promising direction is to integrate supple-
mentary data sets, such as satellite-derived imagery or detailed

File: bridging-the-gap-between-data-reproduction-and-prediction-the-impact-of-feature-selection-and-cross-validation.pdf
Document type: research_paper
Page header: Environmental Science & Technology | pubs.acs.org/est | Article
Page: 10

according to
the range and structure of their respective autocorrelation
patterns. Another promising direction is to integrate supple-
mentary data sets, such as satellite-derived imagery or detailed
meteorological data, to further refine the model’s predictive
capabilities and capture finer-scale variations. Moreover,
exploring alternative feature selection techniques, or hybrid
methods that combine statistical and domain-expertise-driven
approaches, may yield further improvements in model perform-
ance and interpretability, ultimately leading to more reliable air
pollution exposure assessments.

This study demonstrates that aligning model development
with the spatiotemporal structure of mobile air pollution data
and the objective of modeling are critical for reliable predictions.
Among the tested frameworks, models using spatiotemporal CV
with forward feature selection performed best, avoiding
overfitting and producing stable, interpretable UFP maps. In

File: bridging-the-gap-between-data-reproduction-and-prediction-the-impact-of-feature-selection-and-cross-validation.pdf
Document type: research_paper
Page header: Environmental Science & Technology | pubs.acs.org/est | Article
Page: 10

reliable predictions.
Among the tested frameworks, models using spatiotemporal CV
with forward feature selection performed best, avoiding
overfitting and producing stable, interpretable UFP maps. In
contrast, conventional random CV overlooked spatial−tempo-
ral autocorrelation, encouraging data reproduction and unstable
predictions. By focusing on a small set of meaningful predictors
and using rigorous CV, we improved both robustness and
external validity. Feature selection and objective-oriented CV

are essential for building exposure models from mobile data,
offering a reliable path toward high-resolution pollution
mapping in urban environments.

From Data Reproduction to Prediction in Mobile Air Pollution Mapping
Milad Saeedi
Doctor of Philosophy
Department of Civil & Mineral Engineering
University of Toronto
2026
Abstract
Traffic-related air pollution (TRAP) exhibits pronounced spatiotemporal variability within cities,
with steep near-road gradients that are often missed by conventional fixed-site monitoring networks.
Mobile monitoring platforms equipped with high–time-resolution sensors offer a promising means
of capturing this fine-scale variability and supporting high-resolution mapping of urban air qual-
ity. In this thesis, mobile observations of particulate matter (PM2.5), ultrafine particles (UFP),
and black carbon (BC) are collected across the urban road network and integrated with spatial
predictors derived from geographic information systems (GIS), meteorological variables, and tem-
porally varying covariates using land use regression (LUR)–based modeling frameworks to generate
fine-scale exposure surfaces.

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page: 2

geographic information systems (GIS), meteorological variables, and tem-
porally varying covariates using land use regression (LUR)–based modeling frameworks to generate
fine-scale exposure surfaces. However, since mobile air-pollution data are highly structured in space
and time, strong model performance can arise from preprocessing decisions, validation choices, or
other methodological artifacts rather than from relationships that generalize—reflecting the idea
that extensive data manipulation may lead to results that appear to support a desired conclusion.
In the first contribution, the thesis establishes a practical framework for producing city-wide
PM2.5 exposure surfaces from mobile measurements. The analysis demonstrates that low-cost mo-
bile sensing can extend beyond route-level observations to generate coherent urban concentration
patterns, particularly when models incorporate predictors that reflect both near-road influences
and broader background conditions.

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page: 2

nd route-level observations to generate coherent urban concentration
patterns, particularly when models incorporate predictors that reflect both near-road influences
and broader background conditions. Comparisons with regulatory monitoring stations indicate that
the resulting surfaces provide a credible representation of spatial variability suitable for exposure-
oriented applications.
In the second contribution, the thesis demonstrates that the credibility of exposure surfaces de-
pends strongly on the modeling framework, particularly for highly variable pollutants. The analysis
explicitly accounts for data leakage and autocorrelation, and recognizes that different modeling goals
may require different validation strategies and modeling frameworks, showing that the appropriate
framework depends on the intended use of the surface (e.g., reproducing observed measurements
versus producing a product that generalizes to genuinely unobserved locations or conditions). Be-

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page: 2

opriate
framework depends on the intended use of the surface (e.g., reproducing observed measurements
versus producing a product that generalizes to genuinely unobserved locations or conditions). Be-
cause mobile-monitoring observations are autocorrelated, frameworks built around naive random

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page: 3

resampling can allow information leakage between training and testing data, leading to models that
appear highly accurate yet largely reproduce structured artifacts rather than transferable relation-
ships. In contrast, autocorrelation-aware frameworks that enforce separation in space and/or time
and align feature selection and hyperparameter tuning with the same data splits reduce leakage and
overfitting and yield exposure surfaces that are more stable, interpretable, and physically plausible.
These frameworks are also less sensitive to analytical choices such as outlier handling, supporting
more defensible surfaces.
The third contribution investigates how traffic composition and its interannual changes influence
urban air pollution by integrating mobile air-quality monitoring with image-based traffic sensing.
UFP and black carbon (BC) are measured during summer campaigns in 2021 and 2023 using a mobile
platform equipped with sensors and a rooftop 360◦camera.

File: main.pdf

Page: 3

ity monitoring with image-based traffic sensing.
UFP and black carbon (BC) are measured during summer campaigns in 2021 and 2023 using a mobile
platform equipped with sensors and a rooftop 360◦camera. A deep learning object-detection model
is used to quantify multiple vehicle classes from imagery, and LUR models are developed to predict
spatial distributions of both pollutant levels and vehicle-type indicators for each year. Comparing
the resulting surfaces enables identification of persistent and emerging hotspots, evaluation of year-
to-year shifts in traffic-related pollution, and assessment of how fleet composition contributes to
observed spatial patterns beyond what is captured by aggregate traffic indicators alone.
Overall, this dissertation demonstrates an integrated approach for high-resolution TRAP map-
ping that combines mobile sensing, rigorous model evaluation under autocorrelation, and image-
derived traffic characterization.

Chapter 1
Introduction and research objectives
1.1
Background and motivation
Urban air pollution remains a major environmental and public health concern, particularly in cities
where dense transportation networks coincide with high population exposure [1, 2]. A substantial
body of epidemiological research has linked long-term exposure to Traffic Related Air Pollution
(TRAP) with adverse cardiovascular and respiratory outcomes, increased mortality, and broader
societal health burdens [3]. Among traffic-related pollutants, Ultrafine Particles (UFP) and Black
Carbon (BC) are of particular concern due to their strong association with vehicular combustion
emissions, their pronounced spatial heterogeneity at fine spatial scales, and their ability to penetrate
deeply into the respiratory system and contribute to oxidative stress, inflammation, and cardiovascu-
lar and respiratory diseases[4]. Unlike regulated pollutants such as Particulate Matter smaller than

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page: 17

ply into the respiratory system and contribute to oxidative stress, inflammation, and cardiovascu-
lar and respiratory diseases[4]. Unlike regulated pollutants such as Particulate Matter smaller than
2.5 microns (PM2.5), which often reflect regional background conditions, UFP and BC exhibit steep
concentration gradients near roadways and respond rapidly to local traffic activity, fleet composition,
and driving dynamics [5].
Accurate characterization of population exposure to these pollutants therefore requires modeling
approaches capable of resolving spatial variability at the scale of individual road segments and neigh-
borhoods [6]. Conventional fixed-site monitoring networks, while essential for regulatory compliance
and long-term trend analysis, are typically too sparse to capture the fine-scale spatial contrasts
associated with traffic emissions [7]. This limitation has motivated the growing use of mobile mon-

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page: 17

and long-term trend analysis, are typically too sparse to capture the fine-scale spatial contrasts
associated with traffic emissions [7]. This limitation has motivated the growing use of mobile mon-
itoring platforms equipped with compact, high–time-resolution sensors, which enable dense spatial
sampling of urban environments within relatively short time periods [8, 9]. Mobile monitoring has
demonstrated strong potential for revealing near-road pollution gradients and localized hotspots
that are not captured by stationary networks [10].
However, translating mobile measurements into reliable, city-wide exposure surfaces presents
substantial methodological challenges. Mobile datasets are inherently structured in space and time,
with observations clustered along road networks, repeated across limited sampling days, and influ-
enced by transient traffic and meteorological conditions [8]. Spatial air pollution models developed

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page: 17

with observations clustered along road networks, repeated across limited sampling days, and influ-
enced by transient traffic and meteorological conditions [8]. Spatial air pollution models developed
using such data must therefore balance the ability to reproduce observed concentrations with the
capacity to generalize to unmeasured locations and conditions. Land Use Regression (LUR) models
and Machine Learning (ML)–enhanced variants have emerged as widely used tools for this purpose,
as they provide a flexible framework for relating measured concentrations to spatial predictors de-
rived from Geographic Information System (GIS), traffic indicators, and environmental covariates
[11, 12].
Despite their widespread adoption, recent studies have highlighted important limitations in how
spatial air pollution models are commonly trained, validated, and interpreted. In particular, val-
idation strategies that ignore spatial or temporal dependence can lead to information leakage and

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page: 17

ow
spatial air pollution models are commonly trained, validated, and interpreted. In particular, val-
idation strategies that ignore spatial or temporal dependence can lead to information leakage and
overly optimistic estimates of predictive performance [13]. Models may appear highly accurate when

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 1. INTRODUCTION AND RESEARCH OBJECTIVES
Page: 18

evaluated using random data splits, yet fail to generalize to genuinely unobserved locations or time
periods [14]. At the same time, the increasing use of flexible machine learning models introduces
additional challenges related to interpretability and robustness, including the risk that models ex-
ploit spurious correlations or artifacts in structured environmental data rather than learning stable
emission–concentration relationships [15].
Accurate representation of traffic activity represents a further critical constraint in modeling
traffic-related air pollution. Many studies rely on aggregate traffic indicators such as Annual Average
Daily Traffic (AADT), which provide long-term averages but do not capture short-term variation
in vehicle mix or reflect conditions encountered during mobile monitoring campaigns [16].
Yet
emissions of BC and UFP are highly sensitive to traffic composition, with heavy-duty and diesel-

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 1. INTRODUCTION AND RESEARCH OBJECTIVES
Page: 18

ariation
in vehicle mix or reflect conditions encountered during mobile monitoring campaigns [16].
Yet
emissions of BC and UFP are highly sensitive to traffic composition, with heavy-duty and diesel-
powered vehicles contributing disproportionately to local pollutant levels [17]. As a result, spatial
variation in fleet composition can strongly influence observed pollution patterns even where total
traffic volumes are similar [18].
Recent advances in computer vision offer new opportunities to address this gap by enabling di-
rect, data-driven characterization of traffic composition from street-level and 360-degree imagery
[19]. Deep learning–based object detection models, such as those in the YOLO family, allow auto-
mated detection and classification of vehicles at high spatial and temporal resolution [20]. When
integrated with mobile monitoring platforms, these methods provide an independent and physi-

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 1. INTRODUCTION AND RESEARCH OBJECTIVES
Page: 18

ow auto-
mated detection and classification of vehicles at high spatial and temporal resolution [20]. When
integrated with mobile monitoring platforms, these methods provide an independent and physi-
cally interpretable means of capturing traffic activity along sampled road networks, complementing
traditional traffic datasets and improving representation of emission sources [21].
Motivated by these challenges and opportunities, this thesis investigates the integration of mobile
air pollution monitoring, robust spatial modeling frameworks, and image-based traffic composition
estimation to improve high-resolution prediction and interpretation of urban air pollution. Rather
than focusing solely on predictive accuracy, the work emphasizes methodological rigor, appropriate
validation under spatial and temporal dependence, and interpretability of model behavior.
By
systematically examining data preprocessing strategies, modeling approaches, validation designs,

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 1. INTRODUCTION AND RESEARCH OBJECTIVES
Page: 18

propriate
validation under spatial and temporal dependence, and interpretability of model behavior.
By
systematically examining data preprocessing strategies, modeling approaches, validation designs,
and traffic representation, this research aims to advance exposure assessment for traffic-related air
pollutants and support more reliable inference in complex urban environments.
1.2
Problem statement
High-resolution exposure assessment in cities requires concentration surfaces that capture fine-scale
variability driven by transportation activity, yet producing reliable and interpretable maps remains
challenging when measurements are collected using mobile platforms and models are trained on
highly structured spatiotemporal data. In practice, exposure models must simultaneously (i) lever-
age the dense spatial coverage of mobile monitoring, (ii) avoid overfitting and information leakage
caused by spatial/temporal dependence, and (iii) represent traffic emissions in a way that is phys-

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 1. INTRODUCTION AND RESEARCH OBJECTIVES
Page: 18

age the dense spatial coverage of mobile monitoring, (ii) avoid overfitting and information leakage
caused by spatial/temporal dependence, and (iii) represent traffic emissions in a way that is phys-
ically meaningful and interpretable (e.g., accounting for fleet composition rather than only aggre-
gate volume). This thesis addresses this overarching problem through three complementary studies
(Chapters 3–5) that investigate how mobile data collection design, modeling/validation strategy,
and traffic characterization influence the reliability, generalizability, and interpretability of urban
air-pollution prediction surfaces.
1. A fundamental problem is how to transform route-limited and temporally uneven mobile mea-

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 1. INTRODUCTION AND RESEARCH OBJECTIVES
Page: 19

surements into stable, city-wide concentration surfaces suitable for exposure assessment. This
includes selecting appropriate preprocessing and spatial aggregation, identifying predictors
that reflect emissions and dispersion environments, and developing models that remain robust
despite heterogeneous sampling density and limited temporal representativeness.
2. For highly variable pollutants measured with mobile platforms, standard model-development
pipelines (hyperparameter tuning, feature selection, and evaluation under random splits) can
exploit spatial and temporal dependence, leading to information leakage and inflated perfor-
mance. The resulting models may appear accurate while failing to generalize to genuinely
unobserved roads, neighborhoods, or sampling periods. The problem is to design modeling
frameworks workflows that are explicitly aligned with the intended prediction task under spa-
tiotemporal dependence.
3.

3. A persistent limitation in traffic-related pollution modeling is inadequate representation of

1.3
Objectives and research questions
1.3.1
Overall objective
The overall objective of this thesis is to develop and evaluate an integrated framework for high-
resolution urban air pollution mapping using mobile monitoring data, with an emphasis on (i)
producing spatially detailed concentration surfaces for traffic-related pollutants, (ii) ensuring scien-
tifically valid model evaluation under spatiotemporal dependence, and (iii) improving interpretation

Page header: CHAPTER 1. INTRODUCTION AND RESEARCH OBJECTIVES

ing spatially detailed concentration surfaces for traffic-related pollutants, (ii) ensuring scien-
tifically valid model evaluation under spatiotemporal dependence, and (iii) improving interpretation
of observed and predicted pollution patterns through physically meaningful traffic characterization,
including traffic composition derived from imagery.
1.3.2
Research objectives and questions
This research aims to develop robust and generalizable frameworks for predicting traffic-related
air pollutants using mobile monitoring data. The specific objectives and corresponding research
questions are as follows:
1. Develop high-resolution exposure surfaces from mobile monitoring data. How can
route-constrained and temporally uneven mobile measurements be transformed into stable,

city-wide concentration surfaces that capture fine-scale spatial variability relevant for exposure
assessment?
2. Establish modeling and validation frameworks that ensure generalization under
spatial and temporal dependence. How does a modeling framework that aligns model
selection, feature selection, and objective-aware validation with the underlying spatial and
temporal structure of the data affect model performance, robustness, and transferability to
genuinely unobserved locations and conditions?
3. Incorporate traffic composition using computer vision to improve model inter-
pretability and performance. To what extent does integrating high-resolution traffic com-
position derived from street-level imagery improve model interpretability and the ability to
explain spatial variability in traffic-related pollutants, including temporal changes such as
those observed during and after COVID-19?

1.4
Research significance
This dissertation advances high-resolution exposure assessment for traffic-related air pollutants by
addressing key methodological and data limitations that affect the credibility, interpretability, and
predictive usefulness of urban air pollution maps. Using mobile monitoring data and spatial model-
ing, the work develops fine-scale concentration surfaces for particulate matter (PM2.5), black carbon
(BC), and ultrafine particles (UFP), with a particular focus on near-road environments where spatial
variability is pronounced and population exposure is highly heterogeneous. The research demon-
strates the feasibility of producing coherent, city-wide exposure surfaces from mobile measurements

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 1. INTRODUCTION AND RESEARCH OBJECTIVES
Page: 20

tial
variability is pronounced and population exposure is highly heterogeneous. The research demon-
strates the feasibility of producing coherent, city-wide exposure surfaces from mobile measurements
while explicitly confronting the additional challenges posed by highly variable pollutants such as BC
and UFP.
A central contribution of this research is its emphasis on moving beyond data reproduction
toward defensible spatial prediction, while strengthening rigor in spatial model development and
interpretation. Rather than prioritizing agreement with observed measurements under convenient
validation schemes, the dissertation reframes model performance in terms of generalization to gen-
uinely unobserved locations, road segments, and conditions. By explicitly accounting for spatial
and temporal dependence in mobile monitoring data, this work shows that commonly used random
resampling approaches can introduce information leakage and overestimate model performance. In

and temporal dependence in mobile monitoring data, this work shows that commonly used random
resampling approaches can introduce information leakage and overestimate model performance. In
contrast, the proposed modeling and validation frameworks are aligned with realistic prediction ob-
jectives and are complemented by diagnostic and interpretability analyses that reduce the risk of
flexible statistical and machine-learning models exploiting spurious correlations or structured ar-
tifacts in environmental data. Together, these contributions support more transparent inference,
enhance confidence in the physical plausibility of predicted surfaces, and improve the credibility and
transferability of derived exposure maps.
In addition, the work significantly improves representation of traffic composition for understand-
ing exposure surfaces by moving beyond coarse, long-term traffic indicators toward detailed char-
acterization of traffic composition.

Chapter 2
Literature review
Air quality is a critical environmental concern with significant implications for ecosystems and human
health worldwide. Exposure to ambient air pollutants affects millions of people and has been linked
to a wide range of adverse health outcomes. Among these pollutants, Particulate Matter (PM)—
including UFP, BC, and PM2.5—as well as Nitrogen Oxides (NOx) and ozone, are of particular
concern due to their prevalence and toxicity. UFP (particles smaller than 100 nm) and BC exhibit
distinct physical and chemical behaviors compared to larger particulate matter fractions, as they can
penetrate deep into the respiratory system and, in the case of UFP, translocate into the bloodstream,
thereby posing elevated risks to human health [22, 23, 2, 24].
PM2.5 is one of the most extensively studied air pollutants due to its widespread occurrence, abil-
ity to remain suspended in the atmosphere, and well-established associations with adverse health
outcomes.

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page: 22

is one of the most extensively studied air pollutants due to its widespread occurrence, abil-
ity to remain suspended in the atmosphere, and well-established associations with adverse health
outcomes. PM2.5 originates from both primary emissions, such as combustion processes and mechan-
ical abrasion, and secondary formation through atmospheric chemical reactions involving gaseous
precursors including sulfur dioxide, nitrogen oxides, and volatile organic compounds. Due to its size,
PM2.5 can penetrate deep into the lower respiratory tract and has been linked to cardiovascular and
respiratory morbidity, premature mortality, and systemic inflammation [1, 25]. Unlike UFP and BC,
which often exhibit strong local spatial variability driven by traffic activity, PM2.5 typically reflects
a combination of local sources and regional background contributions, resulting in smoother spatial
gradients at the urban scale. This characteristic has made PM2.5 a central pollutant in epidemio-

gradients at the urban scale. This characteristic has made PM2.5 a central pollutant in epidemio-
logical studies and regulatory frameworks, while also posing challenges for source attribution and
high-resolution exposure assessment in complex urban environments[26, 27, 28].
Understanding the spatiotemporal distribution of air pollutant concentrations is essential for
developing effective mitigation strategies and for generating accurate exposure surfaces (i.e., air
pollution maps) used in long-term health assessments [6, 29].
Within urban environments, air
pollution levels exhibit pronounced variability across both space and time, particularly for primary
pollutants such as UFP and BC, which are strongly influenced by local emission sources. Black
carbon, also known as soot, is widely recognized as a marker of diesel exhaust and traffic-related
combustion emissions [4].

A central challenge in epidemiological studies investigating the long-
term health impacts of air pollution is the accurate characterization of population exposure. This
requires reliable methods to measure, model, and predict pollutant concentrations at fine spatial
and temporal scales that reflect real-world human activity patterns [30].
2.1
Traffic-related air pollution
Air pollution originates from a variety of natural and anthropogenic sources; however, road traffic
is a key contributor to air quality degradation in urban environments [31, 32].
In urban areas,
large numbers of vehicles and the close proximity of roadways to human activity result in higher
pollutant concentrations near roads, making traffic-related air pollution (TRAP) an important focus

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 23

of environmental and public health research [33, 34]. Road traffic emissions generate a complex
mixture of gaseous pollutants and particulate matter that are released directly from motor vehicles
or formed through atmospheric transformation processes. This mixture includes primary pollutants
emitted during fuel combustion as well as secondary pollutants produced through chemical reactions
involving traffic-related precursors in the atmosphere [35].
Among traffic-related pollutants, BC and UFP are of particular interest due to their strong as-
sociation with vehicular combustion and their pronounced spatial and temporal variability [27, 36].
Unlike regulated pollutants such as PM2.5, which often reflect broader regional background condi-
tions, BC and UFP respond rapidly to changes in traffic activity, fleet composition, and driving
conditions. These characteristics make them effective tracers of local traffic emissions while simul-

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 23

tions, BC and UFP respond rapidly to changes in traffic activity, fleet composition, and driving
conditions. These characteristics make them effective tracers of local traffic emissions while simul-
taneously posing challenges for accurate characterization using conventional fixed-site monitoring
approaches [37, 38].
Traffic-related emissions originate from both exhaust and non-exhaust sources associated with
vehicle operation and roadway use[39]. Exhaust emissions result from fuel combustion in vehicle
engines and are the primary source of BC and UFP, particularly under high-load or transient driving
conditions[40]. Non-exhaust sources, including brake wear, tire wear, and the resuspension of road
dust, contribute primarily to coarse and fine particulate matter but may also influence local particle
concentrations in heavily trafficked environments[41].
The relative contribution of these sources

concentrations in heavily trafficked environments[41].
The relative contribution of these sources
depends on vehicle technology, fleet composition, roadway characteristics, and driving behavior[42,
43].
Vehicle type and fuel technology play a central role in shaping traffic-related emission profiles[44].
Heavy-duty vehicles, particularly those powered by diesel engines, emit disproportionately higher
levels of BC and UFP compared to light-duty gasoline vehicles[45, 46]. Consequently, traffic com-
position, specifically the proportion of heavy-duty and diesel vehicles, can strongly influence local
pollutant concentrations even when overall traffic volumes are moderate[47]. Emission rates are
further affected by driving dynamics such as congestion, frequent acceleration and deceleration, and

pollutant concentrations even when overall traffic volumes are moderate[47]. Emission rates are
further affected by driving dynamics such as congestion, frequent acceleration and deceleration, and
stop-and-go traffic, which are commonly associated with localized pollution hotspots near intersec-
tions, highway ramps, and signalized road segments[48, 49, 50].
The spatial distribution of BC and UFP in urban environments is characterized by pronounced
heterogeneity and steep near-roadway gradients. Concentrations typically peak adjacent to traffic
corridors and decrease rapidly with increasing distance from the roadway, with the most pronounced
declines often occurring within the first few hundred meters. These spatial patterns are influenced by
traffic intensity, vehicle composition, roadway configuration, and features of the built environment
such as building density, street orientation, and street-canyon geometry, which affect airflow and
pollutant dispersion[51, 52].

such as building density, street orientation, and street-canyon geometry, which affect airflow and
pollutant dispersion[51, 52].
This strong fine-scale variability has important implications for exposure assessment. Fixed-site
monitoring networks, which are generally sparse and designed to represent neighborhood- or city-
scale conditions, often fail to capture localized exposure contrasts associated with traffic emissions.
As a result, individuals living, working, or commuting within the same urban area may experience
substantially different exposure levels depending on proximity to traffic sources. This limitation has
motivated the development and application of high-resolution spatial approaches to better charac-
terize traffic-related pollution patterns and associated health risks[7, 10, 53].
A substantial body of epidemiological evidence has linked exposure to traffic-related air pollu-

tion with adverse health outcomes, including impacts on cardiovascular and respiratory systems,
pregnancy outcomes, and overall mortality. BC is commonly used as a marker of traffic-related
particulate matter and has been associated with inflammatory responses, oxidative stress, and car-
diovascular dysfunction[3, 54]. Collectively, this literature underscores the importance of accurately
characterizing the fine-scale spatial variability of traffic-related air pollution to support robust ex-
posure assessment and inform mitigation strategies.
2.2
Physical and chemical properties
Airborne particulate pollutants differ substantially in their physical and chemical properties, which
influence their atmospheric behavior, spatial distribution, and relevance for exposure assessment.
Key characteristics include particle size, surface area, chemical composition, atmospheric lifetime,
and susceptibility to dispersion and transformation processes. These properties play a central role in

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 24

characteristics include particle size, surface area, chemical composition, atmospheric lifetime,
and susceptibility to dispersion and transformation processes. These properties play a central role in
determining how pollutants are emitted, transported, and measured within urban environments[55,
56].
PM2.5 represents a heterogeneous mixture of solid and liquid components. Owing to their rela-
tively larger size compared to ultrafine particles, PM2.5 particles typically exhibit longer atmospheric
lifetimes and are influenced by both local emission sources and regional background contributions.
As a result, PM2.5 concentrations often display smoother spatial gradients at the urban scale and
are less sensitive to short-term fluctuations in traffic activity. Chemically, PM2.5 includes a range
of constituents such as sulfates, nitrates, organic carbon, elemental carbon, metals, and crustal ma-
terial, reflecting contributions from combustion processes, secondary atmospheric formation, and

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 24

of constituents such as sulfates, nitrates, organic carbon, elemental carbon, metals, and crustal ma-
terial, reflecting contributions from combustion processes, secondary atmospheric formation, and
mechanical sources[57, 58].
UFP are characterized by extremely high number concentrations and large surface area relative
to their mass. These properties make UFP highly reactive and sensitive to atmospheric processes
such as coagulation, condensation, and rapid dilution following emission[59, 60]. Due to their short
atmospheric lifetimes, UFP concentrations typically exhibit strong spatial and temporal variability,
with pronounced gradients near emission sources. In urban environments, UFP are closely associated
with combustion-related activities, particularly road traffic, and respond rapidly to changes in vehicle
activity and driving conditions[61, 62].
BC is a carbonaceous component of particulate matter formed through incomplete combustion
of fossil fuels and biomass.

activity and driving conditions[61, 62].
BC is a carbonaceous component of particulate matter formed through incomplete combustion
of fossil fuels and biomass.
Unlike PM2.5 mass, BC is commonly quantified based on its light-
absorbing properties and serves as a tracer of combustion-related emissions, especially from diesel-
powered vehicles. BC particles often reside within the fine and ultrafine size fractions and exhibit
strong spatial contrasts near traffic corridors. Compared to UFP, BC tends to be more stable in
the atmosphere, allowing it to persist over longer distances while still retaining sensitivity to local
emission sources[63, 62, 64].
The contrasting physical and chemical characteristics of PM2.5, BC, and UFP have important
implications for exposure assessment and modeling. While PM2.5 mass concentrations are effec-
tive for capturing broader spatial patterns and long-term average exposure, BC and UFP provide

important
implications for exposure assessment and modeling. While PM2.5 mass concentrations are effec-
tive for capturing broader spatial patterns and long-term average exposure, BC and UFP provide
enhanced sensitivity to local combustion sources and near-roadway variability. Consequently, in-
tegrating multiple particulate metrics is often necessary to capture the full range of spatial scales
relevant to urban air pollution exposure.
