2.3
Measurement methods
Accurate characterization of particulate air pollution depends on measurement techniques that are
compatible with the physical and chemical properties of the target pollutants. Because PM2.5, BC,
and UFP differ substantially in particle size, mass contribution, and optical behavior, they cannot be
quantified using a single measurement approach. Distinct monitoring methods have therefore been
developed, each with specific strengths and limitations that influence spatial resolution, temporal
coverage, and suitability for exposure assessment[65].
Monitoring of PM2.5 is primarily based on mass concentration measurements and forms the
foundation of regulatory air quality monitoring worldwide. Reference and equivalent methods such
as gravimetric samplers, beta attenuation monitors, and tapered element oscillating microbalances
provide robust long-term measurements and support epidemiological analyses and regulatory com-
pliance.

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 25

s gravimetric samplers, beta attenuation monitors, and tapered element oscillating microbalances
provide robust long-term measurements and support epidemiological analyses and regulatory com-
pliance. However, these instruments are typically deployed at a limited number of fixed locations
and are designed to capture neighborhood- or city-scale conditions rather than fine-scale spatial
variability. Optical particle sensors have increasingly been used to supplement fixed-site networks
and enable denser spatial coverage, although their measurements require careful calibration due to
sensitivity to particle composition and environmental conditions[65, 66, 67, 68].
UFP contribute negligibly to total particulate mass despite dominating particle number concen-
trations. Consequently, UFP cannot be reliably characterized using mass-based monitoring tech-
niques and are instead measured using number-based approaches that are sensitive to nanometer-
scale particles[69, 70].

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 25

equently, UFP cannot be reliably characterized using mass-based monitoring tech-
niques and are instead measured using number-based approaches that are sensitive to nanometer-
scale particles[69, 70]. Condensation particle counters (CPCs) are the most widely used instruments
for measuring total UFP number concentration. In these instruments, sampled air is exposed to
a supersaturated vapor (commonly butanol or water), causing nanometer-sized particles to grow
into larger droplets through condensation. The enlarged particles are then detected and counted
optically as they pass through a laser beam, allowing direct quantification of particle number concen-
tration[71]. Mobility-based instruments provide additional information on particle size by classifying
UFP according to their electrical mobility. Particles are first electrically charged and then passed
through an electric field, where their trajectories depend on particle size and charge. By selectively

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 25

according to their electrical mobility. Particles are first electrically charged and then passed
through an electric field, where their trajectories depend on particle size and charge. By selectively
transmitting particles of specific mobilities to a detector, these instruments generate size-resolved
number distributions. Together, CPCs and mobility-based techniques enable detailed characteriza-
tion of UFP number concentrations and size spectra[72].
Because UFP undergo rapid atmospheric processes such as coagulation, condensation, and di-
lution following emission, measurements are highly sensitive to temporal averaging and sampling
location. These characteristics make UFP instruments particularly effective for capturing short-
term emission dynamics and near-source variability, while also contributing to their limited use in
long-term regulatory monitoring due to cost, operational complexity, and data intensity[73].

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 25

short-
term emission dynamics and near-source variability, while also contributing to their limited use in
long-term regulatory monitoring due to cost, operational complexity, and data intensity[73].
In addition to condensation (CPCs) and mobility-based techniques, diffusion-charging–based
portable sensors have been widely adopted for mobile and personal exposure assessment of UFP. In
these instruments, aerosol particles are exposed to a unipolar corona charger, where nanometer-sized
particles acquire electrical charge proportional to their surface area. Excess ions are subsequently
removed using an ion trap, and the charged aerosol is directed through multiple electrometer stages.
By measuring the electrical current associated with particle deposition in diffusion and filter stages,
these sensors estimate UFP number concentration and, in some designs, an effective mean particle

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 26

diameter.
Owing to their compact size, low power consumption, and high temporal resolution,
diffusion-charging sensors are particularly well suited for mobile monitoring applications, although
they do not provide fully size-resolved particle distributions[74].
Measurement of BC most commonly exploits its strong light-absorbing properties and is typi-
cally performed using optical or thermal-based techniques[75]. Continuous BC monitoring is widely
conducted using optical absorption instruments, such as aethalometers, which estimate BC concen-
tration by measuring the attenuation of light transmitted through particles collected on a filter. As
BC accumulates on the filter, increased light absorption is interpreted as higher BC mass concentra-
tion, allowing high temporal resolution measurements that are particularly well suited for capturing
short-term variability associated with combustion-related emissions[76].

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 26

higher BC mass concentra-
tion, allowing high temporal resolution measurements that are particularly well suited for capturing
short-term variability associated with combustion-related emissions[76].
In addition to optical methods, BC can also be quantified using thermal or thermal–optical
techniques applied to filter samples. These approaches separate carbonaceous aerosol components
based on their volatility at different temperatures, enabling distinction between elemental carbon
and organic carbon fractions. While thermal-based methods provide detailed characterization of
carbonaceous particles, they are typically limited to offline analysis and lower temporal resolution
compared to optical instruments[77].
Optical BC instruments are commonly deployed in both fixed-site and mobile monitoring ap-
plications due to their portability and high time resolution. However, BC measurements can be

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 26

nstruments[77].
Optical BC instruments are commonly deployed in both fixed-site and mobile monitoring ap-
plications due to their portability and high time resolution. However, BC measurements can be
influenced by particle mixing state, coating effects, and instrument-specific assumptions related to
light absorption properties[77]. As a result, careful interpretation is required when comparing BC
concentrations across instruments, environments, or studies[78, 79]. Despite these limitations, BC
monitoring provides a valuable proxy for combustion-related particulate pollution and complements
mass-based PM2.5 and number-based UFP measurements in exposure assessment.
2.4
Traffic composition and pollutant emissions
Traffic composition plays a central role in determining both the magnitude and spatial variability
of traffic-related air pollutant emissions in urban environments. Although total traffic volume is

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 26

s
Traffic composition plays a central role in determining both the magnitude and spatial variability
of traffic-related air pollutant emissions in urban environments. Although total traffic volume is
commonly used as a proxy for emission intensity, pollutant emissions vary substantially across vehicle
classes due to differences in engine technology, fuel type, vehicle mass, and operating conditions.
As a result, aggregate traffic metrics alone may provide an incomplete representation of emission
sources and associated air quality impacts[80, 81, 17].
Emission factors for traffic-related pollutants differ markedly by vehicle class. Heavy-duty ve-
hicles, particularly those powered by diesel engines, generally exhibit substantially higher emission
factors for pollutants such as BC and UFP than light-duty gasoline vehicles. These differences reflect
variations in combustion processes, after-treatment technologies, and engine operating regimes, im-

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 26

tors for pollutants such as BC and UFP than light-duty gasoline vehicles. These differences reflect
variations in combustion processes, after-treatment technologies, and engine operating regimes, im-
plying that even modest fractions of heavy-duty vehicles can contribute disproportionately to total
emissions[40, 82, 83].
Empirical studies have shown that the spatial distribution of BC and UFP in urban environments
is closely associated with freight traffic, buses, and other heavy-duty vehicle activity.
Elevated
concentrations are frequently observed along truck corridors, bus routes, and near logistics hubs,
highlighting the localized influence of vehicle classes with high emission intensities[84, 18, 85].
These effects are particularly pronounced in near-road environments, where emissions occur in

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 27

close proximity to human activity and traffic composition strongly modulates observed pollutant
levels. Vehicle-class–specific information therefore provides a more direct link between traffic activity
and combustion-related pollutant concentrations than aggregate traffic indicators. Incorporating
high-resolution traffic composition data improves identification of dominant emission drivers and
supports interpretation of fine-scale spatial and temporal variability in urban air pollution.
2.5
Temporal changes in urban air pollution
Urban air pollution exhibits substantial temporal variability across multiple time scales, driven by
changes in emission patterns, meteorological conditions, and broader societal factors. Understanding
temporal dynamics is essential for interpreting observed concentration differences across monitoring
campaigns, assessing long-term trends, and ensuring that predictive models capture variability be-
yond purely spatial contrasts.

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 27

for interpreting observed concentration differences across monitoring
campaigns, assessing long-term trends, and ensuring that predictive models capture variability be-
yond purely spatial contrasts. Temporal changes are particularly relevant in studies that integrate
data collected across different years, seasons, or operating conditions[86, 87, 88, 89].
Year-to-year variations in air pollutant concentrations have been observed in many urban environ-
ments, reflecting changes in traffic activity, fleet composition, emission regulations, and background
atmospheric conditions. The COVID-19 pandemic represents a notable example of how abrupt soci-
etal changes can influence urban air quality. Restrictions on mobility, changes in commuting behav-
ior, and reductions in traffic volume during lockdown periods led to measurable changes in pollutant
concentrations in many cities. Traffic-related pollutants exhibited varying responses depending on

Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 27

ior, and reductions in traffic volume during lockdown periods led to measurable changes in pollutant
concentrations in many cities. Traffic-related pollutants exhibited varying responses depending on
local conditions, pollutant type, and the relative contribution of different emission sources. These
disruptions underscore the sensitivity of urban air pollution to changes in traffic activity and empha-
size the need to consider extraordinary temporal events when interpreting multi-year datasets[90,
91].
Seasonal variability is another key driver of temporal changes in urban air pollution. Differences
between summer and winter conditions influence both emission processes and atmospheric behavior.
Seasonal changes in traffic patterns, such as increased idling during colder months or altered travel
behavior, can affect emission rates. In addition, seasonal variation in atmospheric stability, mixing
height, and photochemical activity contributes to differences in observed concentrations.

d travel
behavior, can affect emission rates. In addition, seasonal variation in atmospheric stability, mixing
height, and photochemical activity contributes to differences in observed concentrations. For pol-
lutants such as BC and UFP, seasonal contrasts may be pronounced due to changes in combustion
efficiency, cold-start emissions, and dispersion conditions[92, 93].
Meteorological factors play a central role in shaping temporal variability in air pollutant concen-
trations. Wind speed and direction influence pollutant dispersion and transport, while temperature
affects emission rates, atmospheric chemistry, and boundary-layer dynamics. Low wind speeds and
stable atmospheric conditions are often associated with pollutant accumulation, whereas higher wind
speeds promote dilution. Temperature can also indirectly affect concentrations through its influence
on traffic behavior and energy use. As a result, meteorological variability must be carefully con-

speeds promote dilution. Temperature can also indirectly affect concentrations through its influence
on traffic behavior and energy use. As a result, meteorological variability must be carefully con-
sidered when comparing observations across time or when developing models intended to generalize
across different temporal conditions[94, 95, 96].
Overall, temporal changes in urban air pollution arise from the combined effects of evolving
emission sources, seasonal processes, meteorological variability, and episodic events. These consider-
ations are particularly important for studies that integrate mobile monitoring data collected under
varying temporal conditions or aim to assess changes in pollution patterns over time.

2.6
Air pollution modeling approaches
A wide range of modeling approaches has been developed to estimate spatial and spatiotemporal
variability in ambient air pollutant concentrations, particularly in urban environments where direct
monitoring data are spatially sparse.
These models aim to translate limited measurements into
continuous pollution surfaces that support exposure assessment, epidemiological analyses, and urban
planning applications. Broadly, spatial air pollution modeling approaches can be categorized into
deterministic, statistical, and data-driven frameworks, each characterized by distinct assumptions,
data requirements, and modeling objectives[65].
Deterministic models, including dispersion and chemical transport models, explicitly represent
the physical and chemical processes governing pollutant emission, transport, transformation, and
removal. These approaches rely on detailed emissions inventories, meteorological inputs, and at-

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 28

esent
the physical and chemical processes governing pollutant emission, transport, transformation, and
removal. These approaches rely on detailed emissions inventories, meteorological inputs, and at-
mospheric chemistry parameterizations, and are commonly applied for regional-scale air quality
assessment, regulatory analysis, and policy evaluation. Although physically interpretable, deter-
ministic models are computationally demanding and often operate at spatial resolutions that are
insufficient to capture fine-scale intra-urban variability, particularly in near-road environments[97].
Statistical modeling approaches empirically relate observed pollutant concentrations to spatial
predictors describing land use, traffic, and environmental characteristics. These models are generally
computationally efficient and well suited for urban-scale exposure assessment, with LUR representing
a commonly used framework within this class of models. In parallel, data-driven approaches based on

File: main.pdf

Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW

omputationally efficient and well suited for urban-scale exposure assessment, with LUR representing
a commonly used framework within this class of models. In parallel, data-driven approaches based on
ML have gained increasing attention in spatial air pollution modeling. These methods offer enhanced
flexibility for capturing non-linear relationships and complex interactions among predictors, and can
be applied either independently or as extensions of traditional statistical frameworks[98].
Within this broader modeling landscape, LUR and ML-based approaches have emerged as par-
ticularly relevant for characterizing traffic-related pollutants such as BC and UFP, which exhibit
strong local gradients and are difficult to represent using regional-scale deterministic models. The
following sections describe these two modeling paradigms in greater detail.

provide long-term, continuous measurements of ambient pollutant concentrations. These stations

Air pollution monitoring has traditionally relied on stationary monitoring networks designed to
provide long-term, continuous measurements of ambient pollutant concentrations. These stations
are typically equipped with high-precision instruments and are strategically located to represent
regional background conditions, urban background levels, or specific source-influenced environments.
Stationary monitors play a critical role in regulatory compliance, trend analysis, and epidemiological
studies that require long temporal coverage. However, due to their limited spatial density, stationary
networks often fail to capture fine-scale spatial variability in pollutant concentrations, particularly

This limitation is especially pronounced for pollutants such as UFP and BC, whose concentrations
can vary substantially within tens to hundreds of meters due to localized emission sources and

data may not accurately reflect the conditions experienced by individuals in proximity to roadways
or within complex urban environments. These challenges have motivated the increasing use of mobile
monitoring approaches to complement traditional stationary measurements[102].
Advances in sensor miniaturization and data logging have facilitated the integration of multi-
ple pollutant sensors on mobile platforms, including vehicles, bicycles, and pedestrians[101]. By

Advances in sensor miniaturization and data logging have facilitated the integration of multi-
ple pollutant sensors on mobile platforms, including vehicles, bicycles, and pedestrians[101]. By
sampling pollutants while traversing urban road networks, mobile monitoring enables the character-
ization of spatial variability at much finer resolutions than those achievable with stationary networks
alone. This approach is particularly well suited for mapping traffic-related air pollution, identifying
pollution hotspots, and evaluating exposure patterns along transportation corridors[103].
Compared to stationary monitors, mobile monitoring offers several key advantages.
First, it
allows for high spatial coverage within relatively short time periods, enabling detailed assessment of
intra-urban variability. Second, mobile measurements can be directly linked to roadway characteris-
tics, traffic conditions, and surrounding land use, facilitating the investigation of source–concentration

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 29

rban variability. Second, mobile measurements can be directly linked to roadway characteris-
tics, traffic conditions, and surrounding land use, facilitating the investigation of source–concentration
relationships. Third, mobile platforms can be deployed flexibly and repeatedly, allowing researchers
to target specific areas of interest or to capture variability across different times of day, seasons, or
traffic conditions[104].
Mobile monitoring has been widely applied in previous urban air quality studies to characterize
spatial patterns of traffic-related pollutants. Numerous campaigns have demonstrated the ability
of mobile measurements to reveal sharp concentration gradients near roadways, identify previously

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW

unrecognized hotspots, and improve estimates of population exposure. These studies have employed
a range of platforms and sensor technologies, from research-grade instruments to increasingly so-
phisticated low-cost sensors, reflecting ongoing advancements in mobile sensing capabilities[66, 9].
Despite its advantages, mobile monitoring also presents several challenges related to data quality
and interpretation. Measurements collected from moving platforms are subject to rapid fluctuations
driven by transient emission events, vehicle dynamics, and changing meteorological conditions. As
a result, mobile datasets often exhibit higher variability than stationary measurements and require
careful processing, aggregation, and quality control. Instrument response time, sensor calibration,
and synchronization with location data are particularly important considerations, as delays or inac-
curacies can introduce spatial misalignment or bias in estimated concentrations[102, 105].

Additionally, mobile monitoring campaigns typically provide limited temporal coverage at any
given location compared to stationary monitors. This characteristic necessitates thoughtful study
design and appropriate analytical methods to ensure that observed spatial patterns are represen-
tative and not driven by short-term temporal effects. Repeated sampling, temporal normalization,
and integration with stationary monitoring data are commonly used strategies to address these
limitations[8, 106, 107].
Overall, mobile monitoring represents a powerful complement to traditional stationary air quality
monitoring. When combined with appropriate data processing and modeling approaches, mobile
measurements enable high-resolution characterization of urban air pollution and provide valuable

insights into the spatial structure of traffic-related emissions. These capabilities are particularly
important for pollutants such as UFP and BC, for which fine-scale spatial variability plays a critical
role in determining population exposure and associated health risks.
2.6.2
Predictors for spatial air pollution modeling
Building on the modeling frameworks described above, this section reviews the main categories of
predictors commonly used to characterize spatial variability in urban air pollution. Accurate pre-
diction of pollutant concentrations relies on predictors that represent emission sources, dispersion
processes, and characteristics of the built and natural environment. In spatial air pollution mod-

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 30

diction of pollutant concentrations relies on predictors that represent emission sources, dispersion
processes, and characteristics of the built and natural environment. In spatial air pollution mod-
els, predictors are typically derived from GIS, transportation datasets, meteorological observations,
remote sensing products, and increasingly, image-based data[11, 108, 109].
Traffic-related predictors are among the most influential variables in spatial air pollution mod-
eling, particularly for pollutants such as BC and UFP that are strongly associated with vehicular
emissions. Common traffic indicators include road length by functional class, distance to major
roadways, intersection density, and traffic volume metrics such as AADT. These variables are typi-
cally calculated within multiple buffer distances around monitoring locations to capture the spatial
influence of traffic emissions at different scales. While AADT provides a useful measure of long-

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 30

cally calculated within multiple buffer distances around monitoring locations to capture the spatial
influence of traffic emissions at different scales. While AADT provides a useful measure of long-
term traffic intensity, it often represents average conditions and may not fully capture short-term
variability, fleet composition, or congestion patterns relevant for near-road exposure assessment[16,
110, 111].
Land-use and built-environment predictors provide complementary information related to source
activity and dispersion conditions. Variables such as residential, commercial, industrial, and green
space coverage reflect differences in emission density and human activity patterns, while indicators
of urban form, including building density, street configuration, and land-use mix, influence airflow

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 31

and pollutant dispersion. Population density is also commonly included as a proxy for anthropogenic
activity and potential exposure. Beyond area-based representations of land use and traffic intensity,
spatial air pollution models frequently incorporate proximity- and point-based predictors to represent
localized emission sources and infrastructure[65, 111, 112].
Meteorological predictors play an important role in modulating observed pollutant concentrations
by influencing atmospheric dispersion, dilution, and transformation processes. Variables such as
wind speed, wind direction, temperature, atmospheric stability, and mixing height are frequently
incorporated either directly or indirectly in spatial models. While meteorological conditions are
often treated as temporal covariates, they can also be used to normalize mobile monitoring data or
support the interpretation of spatial patterns derived from short-term sampling campaigns[112, 111,
113].

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 31

treated as temporal covariates, they can also be used to normalize mobile monitoring data or
support the interpretation of spatial patterns derived from short-term sampling campaigns[112, 111,
113].
Remote sensing and geospatial datasets have expanded the range of predictors available for spatial
air pollution modeling. Satellite-derived products, including land cover classifications, vegetation
indices, surface temperature, and nighttime light intensity, provide consistent spatial coverage and
have been used to capture regional background contributions and urbanization patterns.
These
predictors are particularly valuable in areas with limited ground-based monitoring or incomplete
land-use data[114].
More recently, image-based predictors have emerged as a promising extension to traditional
predictor sets, enabling improved characterization of traffic composition and local urban features
that are difficult to capture using conventional datasets[19, 115, 116].

Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW

ng extension to traditional
predictor sets, enabling improved characterization of traffic composition and local urban features
that are difficult to capture using conventional datasets[19, 115, 116].
The relevance and importance of individual predictors vary by pollutant, spatial scale, and
modeling approach. Pollutants dominated by regional background contributions, such as PM2.5,
tend to be more strongly associated with land-use and remote sensing variables, whereas traffic-
related pollutants such as BC and UFP exhibit stronger relationships with traffic and road network
characteristics.
Consequently, effective spatial air pollution modeling often requires integrating
multiple predictor types to capture both local emission sources and broader spatial context. Careful
predictor selection and evaluation are therefore essential to balance model interpretability, predictive
performance, and robustness across different urban environments[12, 117].

2.6.3
Linear-based land use regression (LUR) models
Linear-based LUR models have been widely applied to estimate spatial variability in ambient air pol-
lutant concentrations, particularly in urban environments where direct monitoring data are spatially
sparse. These models relate measured pollutant concentrations to spatial predictors that character-
ize surrounding land use, transportation infrastructure, and other features of the built environment.
By leveraging readily available geographic and environmental data, linear-based LUR models pro-
vide a practical and interpretable framework for generating high-resolution pollution surfaces and
estimating population exposure[66].
Classic linear-based LUR approaches are typically implemented using linear regression, in which

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 31

ble framework for generating high-resolution pollution surfaces and
estimating population exposure[66].
Classic linear-based LUR approaches are typically implemented using linear regression, in which
pollutant concentrations measured at monitoring locations are regressed against spatial predictors
derived within predefined buffer distances. Predictor selection is a central component of this frame-
work and is commonly used to balance interpretability and predictive performance.
The trans-
parency and simplicity of linear-based LUR models have made them particularly well suited for
epidemiological studies that require interpretable exposure estimates[11, 12].

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 32

A defining feature of linear-based LUR modeling is the construction of spatial predictors that
represent emission sources and dispersion environments. Predictors are typically derived from GIS-
based datasets and calculated across multiple buffer distances to capture spatial influence at different
scales. Road network characteristics, such as road length by functional class, intersection density, and
traffic volume within buffers, play a particularly important role in modeling traffic-related pollutants.
Land-use variables, including residential, commercial, industrial, and green space coverage, provide
complementary information related to source activity and dispersion processes[111, 110].
Despite their widespread application, traditional linear-based LUR models exhibit several well-
documented limitations. One key challenge is their reliance on relatively small numbers of stationary
monitoring locations, which may inadequately capture fine-scale spatial variability, particularly for

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 32

mented limitations. One key challenge is their reliance on relatively small numbers of stationary
monitoring locations, which may inadequately capture fine-scale spatial variability, particularly for
pollutants with strong local gradients. In addition, linear model structures may struggle to represent
non-linear relationships, interaction effects, and complex dependencies among spatial predictors.
Temporal variability is often treated implicitly, further limiting the ability of classic LUR models to
represent dynamic pollution processes[118, 119].
Limitations also arise from the spatial resolution and content of conventional GIS-derived pre-
dictors. Predictors prepared at relatively coarse spatial scales may exhibit mismatches with high-
resolution monitoring data, introducing spatial imprecision in estimated concentrations[120]. More-
over, traditional GIS variables often fail to capture fine-scale, street-level features such as traffic com-

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 32

n monitoring data, introducing spatial imprecision in estimated concentrations[120]. More-
over, traditional GIS variables often fail to capture fine-scale, street-level features such as traffic com-
position, roadside infrastructure, and localized built-environment characteristics that can strongly
influence pollutant concentrations[121]. These constraints highlight the need for more flexible and
spatially precise predictor representations and the integration of alternative data sources to better
resolve fine-scale variability[122].
These challenges are particularly relevant for pollutants such as UFP and BC, which exhibit
high spatial variability and strong sensitivity to local traffic conditions. While linear-based LUR
models have been successfully applied to estimate spatial patterns of BC in many urban settings,
their application to UFP has proven more challenging due to the dynamic nature of particle num-

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 32

ed LUR
models have been successfully applied to estimate spatial patterns of BC in many urban settings,
their application to UFP has proven more challenging due to the dynamic nature of particle num-
ber concentrations and their sensitivity to meteorological and traffic-related factors. Nevertheless,
several studies have demonstrated that linear-based LUR models can provide useful estimates of
UFP spatial variability when supported by high-resolution monitoring data and carefully selected
predictors[5, 123, 124].
Overall, linear-based LUR models remain a foundational tool in spatial air pollution research,
offering a balance between interpretability, data availability, and spatial resolution. However, their
reliance on linear regression limits flexibility in representing non-linear effects, interaction structures,
and correlated predictors commonly encountered in spatial datasets[11, 125]. These limitations have

on linear regression limits flexibility in representing non-linear effects, interaction structures,
and correlated predictors commonly encountered in spatial datasets[11, 125]. These limitations have

regression while retaining the conceptual foundation of land use regression modeling.
2.6.4
Machine learning in air pollution prediction
Recent advances in ML have led to the increasing adoption of ML-based LUR models for air pol-
lution prediction, particularly in urban environments characterized by complex emission patterns
and strong spatial heterogeneity. Within this context, machine learning is primarily used to extend
traditional LUR frameworks by enabling non-linear relationships and interactions among land-use
and traffic-related predictors to be learned directly from data. As a result, ML methods have been

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 33

increasingly applied as flexible alternatives to linear regression within the LUR paradigm, rather
than as purely standalone modeling approaches[126, 127, 118].
A variety of ML algorithms have been incorporated into ML-based LUR models, including tree-
based ensemble methods such as Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost) and Random Forests, as
well as neural network approaches such as Artificial Neural Network (ANN). Tree-based models
are particularly well suited for spatial air pollution modeling due to their ability to handle high-
dimensional predictor spaces, capture non-linear effects, and accommodate complex interactions
without requiring explicit functional specification. Neural network models offer additional flexibility,
but typically require larger training datasets and careful tuning to achieve stable and generalizable
performance within LUR-style applications[128, 129, 86].
An important consideration in ML-based LUR modeling is the management of high-dimensional

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 33

d careful tuning to achieve stable and generalizable
performance within LUR-style applications[128, 129, 86].
An important consideration in ML-based LUR modeling is the management of high-dimensional
predictor spaces, which can influence both predictive performance and generalization. While ML-
enhanced LUR models provide increased flexibility compared to linear regression–based approaches,
this flexibility introduces challenges related to model robustness and interpretability[128, 12]. In-
terpretability has therefore emerged as a critical consideration in the application of ML-enhanced
LUR models for environmental exposure assessment. In contrast to traditional LUR models, which
offer transparent coefficient-based interpretation, many ML methods are often viewed as black-box
models.
To address this limitation, post hoc interpretability techniques such as feature impor-
tance metrics and SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) values have been increasingly adopted to

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 33

ack-box
models.
To address this limitation, post hoc interpretability techniques such as feature impor-
tance metrics and SHapley Additive exPlanations (SHAP) values have been increasingly adopted to
support insight into the relative contributions of predictors and the drivers of predicted pollutant
concentrations[130, 131]. Despite their advantages, ML-based LUR models present challenges when
applied to spatial air pollution data. In particular, the risk of overfitting is heightened when models
are trained on spatially clustered observations, underscoring the importance of validation strategies
that explicitly account for spatial dependence[14, 13].
Overall, ML-enhanced LUR models represent a powerful extension of traditional LUR frame-
works, retaining their conceptual foundation while improving the ability to capture non-linear effects
and fine-scale spatial variability. When combined with appropriate validation and interpretability

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis

Page: 33

rks, retaining their conceptual foundation while improving the ability to capture non-linear effects
and fine-scale spatial variability. When combined with appropriate validation and interpretability
strategies, these approaches offer a robust pathway for advancing spatial prediction of traffic-related
pollutants such as UFP and BC.
Feature selection in machine learning–based LUR models
In machine learning, feature selection is a key modeling step used to improve predictive performance,
computational efficiency, and interpretability by identifying a subset of informative predictors from
a potentially high-dimensional feature space.
Although many ML algorithms can accommodate
large numbers of predictors and complex interactions, excessive dimensionality can increase training
time, amplify sensitivity to noise, and reduce generalization, particularly when training data are
limited. Feature selection is therefore commonly employed to control model complexity and mitigate

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 33

g
time, amplify sensitivity to noise, and reduce generalization, particularly when training data are
limited. Feature selection is therefore commonly employed to control model complexity and mitigate
overfitting in ML applications[132, 133, 134, 13]. Figure 2.2 summarizes the primary motivations
for feature selection in machine learning.
In the context of ML-based LUR models for spatial air pollution prediction, feature selection
plays an especially important role due to the high dimensionality and strong correlation structure of
spatial predictors. Predictor sets commonly include variables derived from multiple buffer distances,
land-use categories, transportation networks, meteorological conditions, and emerging data sources

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 34

Figure 2.2: Illustration of the primary motivations for feature selection in machine learning.
such as remote sensing and imagery. While this diversity of predictors enhances model flexibility, it
also increases the risk of redundancy, multicollinearity, and spurious associations, particularly when
the number of monitoring locations is limited relative to the number of candidate predictors[13, 135,
136].
Feature selection methods used in air pollution modeling can be broadly categorized into filter-
based, embedded, and wrapper-based approaches. Filter-based methods apply statistical criteria,
such as correlation thresholds or univariate associations with pollutant concentrations, to remove
weak or redundant predictors prior to model training.
Embedded methods perform feature se-
lection as part of the model fitting process; for example, regularization techniques such as Least
Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator (LASSO) penalize model complexity by shrinking coef-

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 34

e se-
lection as part of the model fitting process; for example, regularization techniques such as Least
Absolute Shrinkage and Selection Operator (LASSO) penalize model complexity by shrinking coef-
ficients of less informative predictors toward zero. Tree-based ensemble models, including Random
Forests and XGBoost, provide intrinsic measures of feature importance based on split frequency or
information gain, enabling identification of predictors that contribute most strongly to model per-
formance. Wrapper-based methods explicitly evaluate subsets of predictors by iteratively training
models and assessing predictive performance under a specified validation scheme. Forward Feature
Selection (FFS), for example, incrementally adds predictors based on their contribution to model
improvement. Although computationally more demanding, wrapper-based approaches allow feature
selection to be directly aligned with the modeling objective and validation strategy, making them

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 34

to model
improvement. Although computationally more demanding, wrapper-based approaches allow feature
selection to be directly aligned with the modeling objective and validation strategy, making them
particularly suitable for spatial air pollution applications where overfitting is a central concern[137,
138, 136].
In spatial air pollution modeling, many predictors are highly correlated due to overlapping buffer
sizes, shared spatial patterns, or common underlying processes (e.g., road length and traffic volume).
Including large numbers of correlated predictors can inflate model complexity without providing
additional explanatory power, leading to unstable predictions and reduced generalizability. Feature
selection therefore serves as a critical mechanism for identifying the most informative predictors and
stabilizing ML-based LUR models in the presence of strong spatial dependence[136, 13, 14].

2.6.5
Model evaluation
Model evaluation is a fundamental component of air pollution modeling, as it determines the extent
to which predicted concentrations accurately represent observed conditions and generalize beyond
the training data. In exposure modeling applications, evaluation results directly influence confidence
in downstream analyses, including spatial mapping, epidemiological inference, and policy-relevant
interpretation.
Consequently, careful consideration of evaluation metrics, validation design, and
diagnostic tools is essential[139, 9, 12].

that compare predicted and observed concentrations. Let yi denote the observed pollutant concen-
tration at location i, ˆyi the corresponding model prediction, ¯y the mean of observed values, and n
the number of evaluation samples.

Beyond numerical performance measures, model evaluation must be aligned with the intended

may prioritize goodness-of-fit, whereas models intended for spatial prediction at unmonitored loca-
tions require strong generalization capability. In spatial air pollution modeling, this distinction is
critical, as high performance under inappropriate validation schemes can mask poor predictive abil-
ity in independent spatial domains.This challenge is amplified when models are trained using mobile

may therefore overemphasize short-term fluctuations or densely sampled areas, rather than meaning-

Cross-validation
Cross-Validation (CV) is a standard framework for estimating predictive performance by repeatedly
partitioning data into training and testing subsets. Its primary objective is to approximate how
a model will perform on unseen data, thereby supporting model comparison, hyperparameter tun-
ing, and feature selection. Conventional CV approaches, such as Random Cross-Validation (RCV),
implicitly assume that observations are independent and identically distributed. While this assump-
tion is reasonable for many classical machine learning tasks, it is frequently violated in datasets
exhibiting structured dependence in space, time, or both[143].
When observations are correlated, random partitioning can result in training and testing sets that

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 36

it is frequently violated in datasets
exhibiting structured dependence in space, time, or both[143].
When observations are correlated, random partitioning can result in training and testing sets that
share information through proximity in space, time, or other latent structures. This phenomenon,
often referred to as information leakage, leads to systematically optimistic performance estimates, as
models are evaluated on data that are not truly independent of the training samples. Roberts et al.
[13] demonstrated that such violations can severely bias model assessment and obscure overfitting,
particularly in spatial, temporal, and spatiotemporal prediction problems[141]. Figure 2.3 illustrates
this issue by contrasting RCV and Spatial Cross-Validation (SCV). Under random partitioning,
training and testing samples are intermingled across the study area, whereas SCV enforces geographic
separation between folds, reducing information leakage and yielding more realistic estimates of spatial

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 36

ing and testing samples are intermingled across the study area, whereas SCV enforces geographic
separation between folds, reducing information leakage and yielding more realistic estimates of spatial
generalization performance.
To address these limitations, alternative validation strategies explicitly align data partitioning
with the intended prediction target. Temporal Cross-Validation (TCV) enforces separation in time
by withholding entire periods during testing, enabling evaluation of a model’s ability to generalize
to unseen temporal conditions. SCV enforces separation in space by withholding geographically
distinct regions or locations, ensuring that predictions are evaluated at spatial domains not repre-
sented during training. More recently, spatiotemporal cross-validation (STCV) strategies have been
developed to jointly address dependence in both space and time. These approaches restrict overlap

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 36

e-
sented during training. More recently, spatiotemporal cross-validation (STCV) strategies have been
developed to jointly address dependence in both space and time. These approaches restrict overlap
between training and testing data in both dimensions, for example by withholding entire locations
during specific time periods[13]. Meyer et al. [14] showed that performance estimates obtained under
RCV can differ substantially from those obtained using target-oriented strategies such as SCV, TCV,
or STCV, revealing overfitting that would otherwise remain undetected. Figure 2.4 summarizes how
different CV strategies can be constructed by enforcing separation across observations, space, time,
or their combination.
By visualizing the structure of training and testing partitions, the figure

Overall, these developments highlight that CV is not merely a technical implementation detail,
but a fundamental modeling decision. In the presence of spatial, temporal, or spatiotemporal depen-
dence, target-oriented validation strategies are essential for obtaining reliable performance estimates
and for avoiding misleading conclusions about model generalizability.

2.7
Challenges in spatial air pollution modeling
Despite substantial advances in sensing technologies and modeling methodologies, spatial air pol-
lution modeling faces several fundamental challenges that complicate inference, evaluation, and
interpretation. These challenges arise primarily from the structured nature of environmental data,
the complexity of urban emission processes, and the increasing reliance on flexible machine learning
models. In particular, spatial autocorrelation, information leakage, and limitations in model inter-
pretability and generalization represent critical issues that must be carefully addressed to ensure
scientifically valid and policy-relevant predictions[145, 146].
2.7.1
Spatial autocorrelation and data leakage
Spatial air pollution data are inherently autocorrelated: observations collected at nearby locations

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 37

ly valid and policy-relevant predictions[145, 146].
2.7.1
Spatial autocorrelation and data leakage
Spatial air pollution data are inherently autocorrelated: observations collected at nearby locations
tend to exhibit similar pollutant concentrations due to shared emission sources, meteorological condi-
tions, and characteristics of the built environment. While this spatial and temporal structure reflects
meaningful environmental processes, it violates the assumption of independent and identically dis-
tributed observations that underpins many standard statistical and machine learning evaluation
procedures[13, 141].

When spatial autocorrelation is not explicitly accounted for, model development and evaluation
can suffer from information leakage. In particular, commonly used random data partitioning strate-
gies may assign spatially proximate observations to both training and testing sets. As a result,
models may be evaluated on data that are not truly independent of the training samples, leading to
overly optimistic estimates of predictive performance. This issue is especially pronounced in dense
urban datasets and in mobile monitoring campaigns, where observations are often clustered along

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 38

to
overly optimistic estimates of predictive performance. This issue is especially pronounced in dense
urban datasets and in mobile monitoring campaigns, where observations are often clustered along
road networks or within specific neighborhoods[147, 141, 148].
The consequences of spatial leakage extend beyond inflated performance metrics. Models trained
and evaluated under inappropriate validation schemes may appear to perform well while failing to
generalize to genuinely unobserved locations. Such failures can undermine the reliability of predicted
pollution surfaces and compromise downstream applications, including exposure assessment and
epidemiological analysis. Addressing spatial autocorrelation therefore requires validation strategies
that explicitly align with the intended prediction task and enforce appropriate separation between
training and evaluation data[13, 149, 15].

2.7.2
Model interpretation, generalization, and the Clever Hans effect
In parallel with challenges related to spatial dependence, the increasing use of machine learning
(ML) models in environmental prediction raises important concerns regarding model interpretability
and generalization. While ML algorithms provide substantial flexibility for capturing non-linear
relationships and complex interactions, this flexibility also increases the risk that models rely on
spurious patterns that do not correspond to underlying physical or environmental processes [15,

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 39

150, 151].
This risk is closely related to the so-called Clever Hans effect, named after a horse in the early 20th
century that appeared capable of performing arithmetic but was later shown to respond instead to
subtle, unintentional cues from its handler (Figure 2.5). In modern machine learning, the Clever Hans
effect refers to situations in which a model produces seemingly accurate predictions by exploiting
unintended correlations or artifacts in the data rather than learning the causal relationships of
interest [152]. Because such behavior is not readily revealed by standard evaluation metrics, it poses
a particular challenge for model interpretation and generalization.
Figure 2.5: Historical illustration of Clever Hans[153], a horse that appeared to perform arithmetic
tasks in the early 20th century. Subsequent investigation revealed that Hans was responding to
subtle, unintentional cues from human observers rather than performing genuine calculations.
Lapuschkin et al.

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 39

in the early 20th century. Subsequent investigation revealed that Hans was responding to
subtle, unintentional cues from human observers rather than performing genuine calculations.
Lapuschkin et al. [152] provide a compelling demonstration of the Clever Hans effect across
multiple machine learning applications. Using explanation techniques such as layer-wise relevance
propagation, they show that models with strong test-set performance can base their predictions on
superficial or irrelevant features. In one illustrative example (Figure 2.6), a classifier trained to
recognize horses in images from the PASCAL VOC dataset relied primarily on a small source tag
embedded in the images, rather than on the visual characteristics of the horse itself. When this
tag was removed, model performance collapsed, while inserting the tag into images of other objects
induced systematic misclassification, revealing that the learned decision logic was fundamentally
non-generalizable.

File: main.pdf

Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 39

induced systematic misclassification, revealing that the learned decision logic was fundamentally
non-generalizable.
In spatial air pollution modeling, analogous Clever Hans–type behavior may arise when mod-
els exploit artifacts introduced by sampling design, predictor construction, spatial clustering, or
correlated covariates. For example, models may implicitly encode location-specific identifiers, road-
network geometry, or campaign-specific characteristics that correlate with pollutant concentrations
but do not represent causal emission or dispersion mechanisms.
Without careful interpretation,
such models may yield plausible spatial predictions while failing to generalize beyond the conditions
under which they were trained.
These concerns highlight the importance of complementing predictive performance metrics with

sible spatial predictions while failing to generalize beyond the conditions
under which they were trained.
These concerns highlight the importance of complementing predictive performance metrics with
interpretability analyses that explicitly examine which information a model uses to generate predic-
tions. Interpretation tools, sensitivity analyses, and spatial diagnostics can help determine whether
learned relationships align with known physical processes or instead reflect Clever Hans–type short-
cuts. Reliable spatial prediction therefore requires not only accurate models, but models whose

This broader risk in data-driven modeling arises when flexible models capture spurious patterns
driven by data structure rather than underlying processes, leading to misleadingly strong perfor-
mance that does not generalize. In the context of spatial air pollution modeling, excessive flexibility
in preprocessing and tuning, when combined with non-independent validation or limited validation
data, can produce models that appear highly accurate yet fail to generalize beyond the sampled
domain. Such behavior reflects not genuine predictive skill, but the imposition of researcher expec-
tations onto structured and correlated data.

2.8
Image-based traffic detection and composition
Recent advances in computer vision and imaging technologies have enabled the use of visual data
as a powerful source of information for characterizing traffic conditions in urban environments. In
contrast to traditional air pollution modeling approaches that rely on aggregated or static traffic
indicators, image-based methods allow direct observation of vehicles and road activity at fine spatial
and temporal scales. As a result, imagery has become an increasingly important data source for
estimating traffic volume and traffic composition in complex urban settings[154, 155].
Imagery used for traffic analysis can originate from a range of platforms, including fixed roadside

estimating traffic volume and traffic composition in complex urban settings[154, 155].
Imagery used for traffic analysis can originate from a range of platforms, including fixed roadside
cameras, aerial and satellite systems, and increasingly, street-level and 360-degree mobile imaging
platforms. Street-level imagery provides detailed views of vehicles, lane structure, and surrounding
built environments, while mobile imaging systems offer continuous spatial coverage along road net-
works. When combined with precise location information, these data enable spatially explicit char-
acterization of traffic patterns that are difficult to capture using conventional GIS-based datasets[19,
156, 155, 157, 158].
The automated detection and classification of vehicles in images has been greatly advanced by the
development of deep learning–based object detection models. Early traffic monitoring approaches

2.9
Gaps in existing literature
Despite extensive research on traffic-related air pollution, several important gaps remain in the
current literature, particularly with respect to high-resolution exposure assessment, representation
of traffic composition, and model generalization.

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 42

pollution, several important gaps remain in the
current literature, particularly with respect to high-resolution exposure assessment, representation
of traffic composition, and model generalization.
First, although numerous studies have documented the strong influence of traffic on urban air
pollution, most spatial modeling frameworks rely on aggregate traffic indicators such as road length
or AADT. These metrics do not explicitly capture traffic composition, despite well-established dif-
ferences in emission profiles between light-duty and heavy-duty vehicles. As a result, many existing
models are limited in their ability to distinguish emission hotspots driven by freight and service vehi-
cles from those dominated by passenger cars, especially in near-road and street-level environments.
Second, while mobile monitoring has substantially improved the characterization of fine-scale
spatial variability in pollutants such as BC and UFP, integration of mobile measurements with

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 42

vironments.
Second, while mobile monitoring has substantially improved the characterization of fine-scale
spatial variability in pollutants such as BC and UFP, integration of mobile measurements with
vehicle-type–specific traffic information remains limited. Traditional traffic datasets often lack suffi-
cient spatial resolution or vehicle-class detail to fully exploit the potential of mobile air quality data.
Consequently, the link between observed pollutant patterns and underlying traffic composition is
often inferred indirectly rather than quantified explicitly.
Third, recent advances in computer vision have enabled automated vehicle detection from street-
level imagery, yet these methods have primarily been applied in transportation engineering and urban
analytics rather than in air pollution exposure modeling. Existing air quality studies that incorporate
imagery typically use visual data as generic predictors or contextual indicators, with relatively few

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW
Page: 42

ics rather than in air pollution exposure modeling. Existing air quality studies that incorporate
imagery typically use visual data as generic predictors or contextual indicators, with relatively few
studies transforming imagery into physically interpretable traffic composition variables that can be
directly integrated into established modeling frameworks such as LUR.
Fourth, temporal changes in traffic composition and their implications for air pollution patterns
remain insufficiently explored. While several studies have examined year-to-year or seasonal changes
in pollutant concentrations, fewer have jointly modeled changes in both air pollution and vehicle-
type–specific traffic activity using consistent spatial frameworks. This limits understanding of how
shifts in fleet composition, such as post-pandemic changes in freight and delivery traffic, translate
into evolving pollution hotspots.

File: main.pdf

Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW

consistent spatial frameworks. This limits understanding of how
shifts in fleet composition, such as post-pandemic changes in freight and delivery traffic, translate
into evolving pollution hotspots.
Finally, the increasing use of flexible machine learning models in spatial air pollution modeling
has raised concerns regarding overfitting, spatial data leakage, and model interpretability. Many
studies report strong predictive performance using random validation strategies that do not ade-
quately account for spatial or spatiotemporal dependence, potentially overstating model generaliza-
tion. There remains a need for modeling frameworks that combine high-resolution data sources with
validation strategies explicitly designed to mitigate spatial and temporal leakage, while preserving

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 2. LITERATURE REVIEW

interpretability and physical relevance.
Collectively, these gaps highlight the need for integrated approaches that (i) explicitly represent
traffic composition at high spatial resolution, (ii) leverage mobile monitoring data to capture fine-
scale pollutant variability, (iii) use image-based methods to generate interpretable traffic indicators
rather than opaque predictors, (iv) examine temporal changes in both traffic activity and air pollu-
tion, and (v) employ robust modeling and validation strategies that prioritize generalization. The
studies presented in this dissertation directly address these gaps.

3.1
Chapter overview
PM2.5 has significant impacts on human health, making it essential to understand its spatial and
temporal variations. This study focuses on developing LUR models and improving their perfor-
mance in predicting PM2.5 concentrations in an urban setting. In this study, air quality data were
collected using a sensor on a courier truck in downtown Toronto. XGBoost, a machine learning
algorithm, was employed to address limitations in traditional linear regression-based LUR models,
incorporating predictors such as land use, meteorology, and emissions to build robust models. A
total of 27 models were trained, with varying road segment lengths, predictors, and outlier treatment
thresholds. Three models tested the impact of road segment length on model predictions. Eight

File: main.pdf

. A

thresholds. Three models tested the impact of road segment length on model predictions. Eight
models examined the effect of removing outliers with different thresholds, revealing that appropri-
ate thresholds improve accuracy. Ten models assessed the addition of emission and traffic data,
which did not enhance performance, likely due to overlapping effects with other predictors. In six
models, time-variant predictors such as time of day, month, humidity, wind speed, temperature,
and pollutant concentrations from stationary stations were included. Adding these predictors sig-
nificantly improved model performance, highlighting the complex relationships in LUR models for

3.2
Introduction
Air quality stands as a critical environmental concern, impacting millions of individuals worldwide.
Among the air pollutants, PM2.5 severely affects human health[163].Understanding spatial or spa-
tiotemporal pollutant concentrations plays a vital role in implementing effective mitigation strategies
and in developing accurate exposure surfaces to support the analysis of long-term health effect [6].
Reference air quality stations provide reliable air pollutant concentration information, but they have
limitations due to data being available at limited points[163]. To overcome these limitations, re-
searchers use models such as geostatistical interpolation, photo-chemical dispersion, and LUR to
assess air pollution exposure and its associated health effects in urban areas[164]. While geostatis-

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis

searchers use models such as geostatistical interpolation, photo-chemical dispersion, and LUR to
assess air pollution exposure and its associated health effects in urban areas[164]. While geostatis-
tical interpolation and dispersion models are valuable, their resolution is often coarse, making them
less suitable for capturing small-scale urban variability that is crucial in exposure assessment[8]. In
contrast, LUR models have become popular for quantifying intraurban variation in air pollutant
concentrations, offering simplicity and the ability to predict fine-scale pollution variations that can
reduce exposure measurement errors[8, 165]. The main components of LUR models include monitor-

spatial variations in pollutant concentrations. Mobile monitoring offers an alternative with better
spatial coverage, though it presents challenges in addressing temporal variability[166, 105].
Criticism of linear regression based LUR models includes limited flexibility and difficulty in
incorporating highly correlated predictors [167]. Studies have explored machine learning models,
such as neural networks, support vector machine, and tree-based models, which improve accuracy
and handle multicollinearity[167, 168]. Predictors for LUR models include land use, meteorology,
pollutant emissions, and built environment characteristics. Common predictor variables are traffic,
population density, land use, topography, and location[127].

LUR models are widely used in air quality research to estimate pollutant concentrations in areas
lacking direct monitoring data. By incorporating spatial predictors such as land use types, traffic
density, population distribution, and meteorological data, LUR models can effectively predict pollu-
tion levels beyond measurement locations (whether fixed or mobile), including off-road environments.
For instance, Padhi et al.[173] discusses the application of LUR models in air pollution assessment,
highlighting their effectiveness in capturing spatial variability.
Similarly, research by Hankey et

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 3. URBAN AIR POLLUTION DATA COLLECTION, MAPPING, AND ...
Page: 46

al.[174] demonstrates the successful modeling of on-road particulate air pollution, including particle
number, BC, and PM2.5, using LUR techniques based on mobile monitoring data. Their findings
highlight the effectiveness of LUR models in capturing spatial variability of pollutants across urban
settings. While uncertainties may arise when extrapolating to areas far from monitoring points, LUR
models have been validated in diverse settings, demonstrating their robustness in capturing spatial
trends across urban and suburban environments. These strengths make LUR models valuable for
understanding exposure in off-road locations.
In this study, we used the XGBoost algorithm to develop multiple models with the aim of
predicting concentrations of PM2.5. To gather the data for developing the models, a sensor was
installed on a courier truck operating in Toronto, Canada.
The primary focus of this research

the aim of
predicting concentrations of PM2.5. To gather the data for developing the models, a sensor was
installed on a courier truck operating in Toronto, Canada.
The primary focus of this research
is to investigate the possibility of enhancing prediction ability of the model by employing diverse
predictors and addressing outliers. By constructing distinct models for varied sets of input data as
predictors, we conducted comparisons to understand the impact of these predictors and modifications
on the overall performance of the model. Additionally, the feasibility of utilizing delivery vehicle
routes for constructing a LUR model to predict exposure surfaces was assessed. The study outcomes
can be leveraged to investigate the potential of a fleet serving as a mobile monitoring platform.
Beyond contributing to the methodological development of exposure surfaces, this study offers
valuable insights for urban planning, public health, and community-level air quality management.

Beyond contributing to the methodological development of exposure surfaces, this study offers
valuable insights for urban planning, public health, and community-level air quality management.
Previous research has demonstrated the value of LUR models in identifying urban air pollution
hotspots and supporting interventions to mitigate health risks associated with air pollution. For
instance, Hoek et al.[11] reviewed the application of LUR models in air pollution epidemiology,
highlighting their effectiveness in exposure assessment and their potential to inform public health
policies. Similarly, Jerrett et al.[175] used LUR models to examine the relationship between air pollu-
tion and mortality in urban areas, underscoring their role in exposure analysis and public health risk
assessment. Furthermore, Lu et al.[176] discussed the application of LUR models for identifying air
pollution hotspots in urban environments, emphasizing their value in supporting population health

across urban areas, which can help inform effective strategies to reduce population exposure and
promote healthier urban spaces.
3.3
Methods
3.3.1
Study area
This study is set in the city of Toronto, located on the north shore of Lake Ontario in southeastern
Canada and has a population of over 2.7 million, making it the largest city in the country. Toronto
experiences a temperate climate with distinct seasons. This city has a relatively flat topography,
especially in the downtown core and along the lakeshore. Air quality in Toronto can vary depending

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 3. URBAN AIR POLLUTION DATA COLLECTION, MAPPING, AND ...
Page: 46

o
experiences a temperate climate with distinct seasons. This city has a relatively flat topography,
especially in the downtown core and along the lakeshore. Air quality in Toronto can vary depending
on several factors, including weather, traffic, industrial activity, seasonal influences, and proximity
to major sources like Toronto’s municipal expressways and provincial highways or its two airports:
Toronto Pearson International Airport located west of the city and Billy Bishop Toronto City Air-
port on the Toronto Islands.
Among all the sources, traffic emissions are a major local source

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 3. URBAN AIR POLLUTION DATA COLLECTION, MAPPING, AND ...
Page: 47

of air pollution in Toronto. This includes emissions from cars, trucks, buses, and other forms of
transportation.
3.3.2
Data collection and preprocessing
We conducted a mobile monitoring campaign in Toronto by installing a sensor on a truck operated by
Purolator, a courier and package delivery company in Toronto, to capture spatiotemporal variation
of ambient PM2.5. The mobile monitoring campaign took place between June 21, 2022, to January
11, 2023. The sensor, manufactured by Geotab, a telematics and fleet management company, uses
a Honeywell HPMA 11S50 low-cost sensor. The HPMA 11S50 sensor is capable of measuring ambi-
ent PM concentrations. It can detect and quantify real-time PM2.5 and Particulate Matter smaller
than 10 microns (PM10). In fact, the sensor is designed to capture a broad spectrum of particulate
concentrations, suitable for urban air quality monitoring. It responds rapidly to changes in PM2.5
levels, ensuring near real-time data collection.

levels, ensuring near real-time data collection. The sensor operates reliably across a wide range of
temperatures and humidity conditions. It was factory-calibrated to ensure accurate and consistent
measurements[177, 178]. This sensor is integrated into a Geotab unit which also includes a SGX Sen-
sortech MiCS-4514 to collect Nitrogen Dioxide (NO2) concentrations and Bosch Sensortech BME280
to collect temperature, humidity, and atmospheric pressure[168]. Moreover, Geotab reports a wide
range of vehicle telematics data such as GPS location, fuel level, and other on-board diagnostics
(OBD). The sensor reports a record whenever a property changes, for example whenever PM2.5 con-

prior to installation.

Each data point consists of a pair of x, y coordinates associated with a PM2.5 concentration.
For this reason, we averaged all observations corresponding with each road segment, defining those
segments using different methods. In the first method, the observations were aggregated for each
road segment (defined from one intersection to another) from a road network shapefile for Toronto.

ose
segments using different methods. In the first method, the observations were aggregated for each
road segment (defined from one intersection to another) from a road network shapefile for Toronto.
Then the center of each road was identified, and buffers were created around each point to compute
model predictors. As a result, all data points were assigned to approximately 1,050 road segments.
In the second and third methods, segments were defined every 50-meter and 100-meter intervals,
with the average concentration computed for all readings associated with these segments. With a
50-meter aggregation, the total number of segments is 1,673, and with a 100-meter aggregation, it
is 705. These three methods were used to evaluate the impact of spatial aggregation. We used the
50-meter segment as the base case, against which all models will be compared since it refers to the
highest resolution. These three methods of aggregation have been employed by other studies[8, 166,
179].

3.3.3
Generation of predictor variables
Several LUR models were developed using different sets of predictors to evaluate the impact of
choosing predictors on the performance of LUR models.
We divided our predictors into three
categories including: specific to a location predictor, traffic related predictors, as well as weather
and air quality related predictors which can change temporally. To calculate predictors, varying
buffer sizes (50, 100, 200, 250, 300, 500, 1000, 2000 meters) were created around each segment mid-
point (based on the three methods for data aggregation). The calculation of predictors involved the
calculation of area, length, or number of land use types and traffic related variables within each of
these buffers, computation of distances to important places in the city from the buffers’ centers, and
temporally changing variables (weather and regional air quality).
Predictors that accurately explain emissions or traffic can affect LUR models and their effec-

File: main.pdf
Document type: thesis
Page header: CHAPTER 3. URBAN AIR POLLUTION DATA COLLECTION, MAPPING, AND ...
Page: 49

he city from the buffers’ centers, and
temporally changing variables (weather and regional air quality).
Predictors that accurately explain emissions or traffic can affect LUR models and their effec-
tiveness in estimating pollution concentrations[167]. In this study, LUR models were created to
assess the contrasting effects of utilizing two data sources for traffic and emissions. In one model,
traffic volume and emissions for NOx from the GTAModel, an activity-based model for the Greater
Toronto Area which uses the A Multimodal Transportation Planning Software Package (EMME)
traffic assignment package, were included as predictors[180, 181]. Then, four models were developed
by including AADT data instead of emission data. The AADT data used in this study was devel-
oped by Ganji et al.[162] by using traffic count data to calculate AADT instead of a travel demand
model. The AADT data was improved by Ganji et al.[182] using aerial imagery. Four models were

Land-use regression modelling
In this study, the XGBoost model, which is a tree-based machine learning method, was trained using
different sets of predictors to predict long-term PM2.5 exposure surfaces and to study the impact
of using different sets of predictors on the model performance.

ine learning method, was trained using

from this model using different feature importance measures. In this study, The Python libraries

After tuning and choosing the best model, we used a feature importance method to assess the impact

To assess model accuracy, the dataset was partitioned into training and testing sets, with 20%
allocated for testing. The model was trained and evaluated at the same time using a 10-fold CV
approach in combination with grid search for hyperparameter tuning. In fact, by using this method,
the training data set was divided into 10 subsets, and the model was trained and evaluated 10 times.
Each time, one of the 10-folds is used for testing the model and other folds for training. The model
performance was averaged for all these 10 models to provide an overall assessment of its accuracy
and generalization. After finding the best models and their hyperparameters, the models were tested

3.4
Results
3.4.1
Sensor performance
The comparison between the PM2.5 values recorded by the mobile sensor and the regulatory moni-
toring station yielded an R2 value of 0.63. This suggests reasonable alignment between the mobile
and stationary measurements, though variability is still present. A significant factor influencing this
correlation is the number of records per minute and the proximity of the truck to the station, as only
records collected within a 200-meter radius of the station were included in this comparison. Min-
utes with a higher density of second-by-second records tend to show averaged readings closer to the
station’s data, as more frequent measurements reduce random fluctuations (Figure 3.3). Conversely,
minutes with fewer records or greater distances may be more impacted by transient conditions,
leading to less consistent alignment with the stationary measurements.

leading to less consistent alignment with the stationary measurements.
